questions.questionText:
The Australia–Canada–India Technology and
Innovation (ACITI) Alliance was announced on the sidelines of which summit?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Australia–Canada–India Technology and Innovation** (ACITI) Alliance was **announced on the** **sidelines of the G20 Leaders’ Summit held in Johannesburg**. This trilateral partnership aims to enhance cooperation among the three democracies-Australia, Canada, and India, across emerging and critical technologies, including clean energy, resilient supply chains (especially for critical minerals), and artificial intelligence.
● The ACITI Partnership is significant as it marks a strategic realignment to collaborate on technology innovation and supply-chain security amid a multipolar global technology landscape. It reflects a **shift** **from broad multilateralism to agile minilateral coalitions** focused on **technological sovereignty** and securing a sustainable and secure future in the digital economy. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Recently, Australia-Canada-India Technology and Innovation (ACITI) Partnership was announced on the sidelines of the G20 Summit in Johannesburg.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: Biology and Biotechnology
questions.questionText:
In which of the following countries is Wild
Poliovirus (WPV) still considered endemic?
I. - Indonesia
II.	 Pakistan
III.	 Afghanistan
IV.	 Nigeria
V. - Sudan
Select the correct answer using the code given below:

options.b: I, III, IV, and V only

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Poliomyelitis (polio)** is a highly **infectious viral disease** that largely affects children under 5 years of age. The virus is **transmitted by person-to-person** spread mainly through the faecal-oral route or, less frequently, by a common vehicle (e.g. contaminated water or food) and **multiplies in the intestine**, from where it can **invade the nervous system and cause paralysis**.
● Wild poliovirus cases have **decreased by over 99% since 1988**, from an estimated 350 000 cases in more than 125 endemic countries to 6 reported cases in 2021. Of the 3 strains of wild poliovirus (type 1, type 2 and type 3), wild poliovirus **type 2 was eradicated in 1999 and wild poliovirus type 3 was eradicated** **in 2020.**
● As of 2022, **endemic wild poliovirus type 1** remains in two countries:  **Pakistan and Afghanistan. So,** **points II and III are correct.**
● Indonesia **is not an endemic country for wild poliovirus**. It was declared polio-free in **2014**. Indonesia has reported an outbreak of poliovirus type 2, in 2022. It arose from years of low polio immunization coverage. Since June 2024, no poliovirus has been detected in children or the environment. Therefore, recently the World Health Organisation (WHO) has officially declared the **end of Indonesia’s poliovirus** **type-2 outbreak**.
● Nigeria **was declared free of wild poliovirus in 2020** and is no longer considered endemic. It was the last African country to eliminate WPV. **So, point IV is not  correct.**
● Sudan is **not an endemic WPV country**. It has eliminated wild poliovirus in 2020 but has experienced outbreaks of circulating vaccine-derived poliovirus type 2 (cVDPV2), which is different from endemic WPV. **So, point V is not correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Recently, Indonesia has been declared polio-free by WHO.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
questions.microTopic: President, Vice President, Governor
questions.questionText:
With reference to the recent opinion on the
Presidential Reference by the Supreme Court, consider the following statements:
I. - The Court held that it cannot impose strict timelines on Governors or the President for granting assent to State Bills.
II.	 The President is constitutionally required
to consult the Supreme Court whenever a Governor reserves a Bill for consideration under Article 201.
III.	 The exercise of the Governor’s discretion
under Article 200 is not subject to judicial review, except in cases of unreasonable and indefinite inaction.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● In 2025, the **Supreme Court gave an advisory opinion** under **Article 143**, clarifying the scope of **powers** **of the Governor and President under Article 200 and 201**. This reference was triggered after conflicting interpretations regarding whether courts can prescribe specific time limits for assent.
● The Supreme Court  stated that **it is not appropriate to impose judicially mandated timelines** for Governors (Article 200) or the President (Article 201) because **the Constitution is silent** on any specific time limits. Therefore, the Court refused to fix any definite period for assent, withholding, or reservation of Bills. **So, statement I is correct.**
● The Court clarified that **the President is** **not** **required to consult the Supreme Court** when a Governor reserves a Bill. Under Article 201, the President decides based on her **own subjective satisfaction**. **So,** **statement II is not correct.**
● The Court held that while the Governor’s actions under Article 200 are generally **not justiciable**, the judiciary has a **limited power** to intervene **only** when there is indefinite  inaction. In such exceptional cases, the Court may issue a **mandamus** directing the Governor to decide within a reasonable time. **So,** **statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
● **Article 200** of the Indian Constitution deals with the **Governor’s options** when a State Bill is presented for assent. The Governor may:
1. **Assent** to the Bill,
2. **Withhold assent**,
3. **Return the Bill** (if not a Money Bill) to the State Legislature for reconsideration, or
4. **Reserve the Bill for consideration of the President**.
● **Article 201** concerns the **President’s role** when a Bill is reserved by the Governor. The President may:
1. **Assent**,
2. **Withhold assent**, or
3. **Return the Bill** (if not a Money Bill) for reconsideration by the State Legislature.
● **Article 143** provides for the **advisory jurisdiction of the Supreme Court**. Under this Article, the President can seek the Court’s opinion on: Questions of **law or fact** of public importance, or Disputes related to **pre-constitution treaties or agreements**. The Supreme Court’s opinion under Article 143 is **advisory** and not binding. However, it helps the Union resolve constitutional doubts.
**Relevance:** Recently, the Supreme Court has issued a significant advisory opinion under Article 143 of the Constitution, responding to a Presidential reference.

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.microTopic: Security and Strategic Issues (Terrorism, Cyber, Maritime, Exercises, etc.)
questions.questionText:
With reference to the Colombo Security Conclave
(CSC), consider the following statements:
I. - It was formally institutionalised in 2024.
II.	 Both Bangladesh and Seychelles are its
founding members.
III.	 All CSC members are members of the Indian
Ocean Rim Association (IORA), and the Indian Ocean Naval Symposium (IONS).
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Colombo Security Conclave (CSC)** is a regional security grouping in the Indian Ocean Region. It brings together National Security Advisers of member countries to strengthen cooperation on maritime security, counter-terrorism, cyber security, and other transnational threats.
● The **Founding Documents of the CSC were signed in Sri Lanka in August 2024**, formalising the grouping and giving it a structured institutional base. **So, statement I is correct.**
● Initially beginning as a trilateral maritime cooperation mechanism, the CSC was expanded and formalised in recent years. Recently, in **2025**, **Seychelles** took a decision to **accede to the CSC as a full member**, whereas **Bangladesh became the fifth member state of the CSC in 2024**. The CSC now comprises India, Sri Lanka, Maldives, Mauritius, Bangladesh, and Seychelles. **So, statement II is not correct.**
● The CSC countries are all members of the two region-wide Indian Ocean groupings, the Indian Ocean Rim Association (IORA), and the Indian Ocean Naval Symposium (IONS). **So, statement III is correct.**
● CSC is a regional security grouping with its core objective to **promote regional securit**y by addressing **transnational threats** and challenges of common concern to the member states. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:**  7th NSA-Level Meeting of the Colombo Security Conclave  was held recently

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: Biology and Biotechnology
questions.questionText: BIRSA-101, recently seen in the news, refers to:

options.a:
A new AI platform launched by the Ministry
of Home Affairs for tracking financial cybercrimes across states
options.b:
A tribal empowerment programme aimed
at preserving indigenous culture and forest rights
options.c:
A tribal welfare index introduced by NITI
Aayog to assess socio-economic development in forest regions
options.d:
A new indigenous CRISPR-based gene
therapy developed for treating Sickle Cell Disease in India

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● CRISPR (Clustered Regularly Interspaced Short Palindromic Repeats) is an advanced genome-editing technology used to precisely modify defective genes.
● India recently launched its **first indigenous CRISPR-based gene therapy**, named **BIRSA-101**, to treat **Sickle Cell Disease (SCD)**, a genetic blood disorder that disproportionately affects tribal communities in central and eastern India. This innovation marks a milestone in India’s genomic medicine capabilities and makes advanced gene therapy more affordable.
● It is developed by **CSIR–Institute of Genomics and Integrative Biology (IGIB)**. It works by correcting the genetic mutation responsible for sickle cell disease and is administered as a **one-time infusion**. It is named after **Birsa Munda** on his 150th birth anniversary, symbolising its significance for tribal communities. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**Birsa Munda:**
● Belonged to the **Munda tribe** of the **Chhotanagpur Plateau** (present-day Jharkhand).
● Led a major **tribal mass movement** against British policies in the Bihar–Jharkhand region in the late 19th century.
● Opposed **British land-grabbing**, bonded labour, and exploitation of tribal communities.
● Motivated tribals to **assert land rights** and revive their **traditional customs** and identity.
● Led the **Munda Rebellion (Ulgulan / Tamar Revolt)** against oppressive forest laws and heavy taxes.
● United several tribal groups under the idea of a **Munda Raj** and adopted **guerrilla** **warfare** tactics.
● Revered as **“Bhagwan” or “Birsa Bhagwan”** by followers for his leadership.
● He drew inspiration from **Munda traditions, Christianity, and Hinduism**, guiding people through blended rituals.
● His movement contributed to the passing of the **Chotanagpur Tenancy Act, 1908**, protecting tribal land from transfer to non-tribals.
● He founded the **faith of Birsait**, a blend of animism and indigenous beliefs, which emphasized the worship of a single god.
● He became their leader and was given the nickname, **‘Dharti Aba’ o**r the father of the earth.
● **Jharkhand state** was created on his birth anniversary (15 November) in **2000** in his honour. His birth anniversary, **15 November**, was declared **Janjatiya Gaurav Divas** in 2021.
**Relevance:** Recently, India launched the first indigenous CRISPR gene therapy for Sickle Cell Disease.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Industry (Waste Materials, etc.)
questions.questionText:
With reference to Viscose Staple Fibre (VSF),
consider the following statements:
I. - It is a biodegradable fibre produced by regenerating natural cellulose such as wood pulp.
II.	 It has properties similar to cotton, including
high moisture absorbency and breathability.
III. It does not blend well with other fibres due to
its rigid chemical structure.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Viscose Staple Fibre (VSF)** is an important textile raw material widely used in India’s apparel and home furnishing sectors. As a regenerated cellulose fibre, it combines the comfort characteristics of natural fibres with the versatility of man-made fibres. The recent revocation of the Quality Control Order (QCO) aims to improve raw material access and support the textile industry’s growth.
● VSF is derived from natural sources like **wood pulp or cotton linter**, which are processed chemically to regenerate cellulose into fibre form. Since its raw material is plant-based, VSF is **biodegradable** and environmentally friendlier than many synthetic fibres. **So, statement I is correct.**
● VSF is often used as a **substitute for cotton** because it shares many similar characteristics such as **softness, high moisture absorbency, breathability, and comfort**. These qualities make it suitable for apparel, innerwear, and home textiles. **So, statement II is correct.**
● VSF is known for being a **highly versatile fibre** that **blends very well** with other materials such as cotton, polyester, wool, and acrylic. It is valued in the textile industry precisely because of its adaptability in blended fabrics. Its chemical structure is **not rigid**, and its flexibility allows easy blending. **So, statement** **III is not correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Recently, the Central Government has revoked the Quality Control Order (QCO) requirement for Viscose Staple Fibre (VSF) with immediate effect.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Cyclones
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Tropical Storm Landfall Country
I. - Melissa Mexico
II. - Montha Bangladesh
III. - Kalmaegi Vietnam
IV. - Fung-wong Philippines
How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Hurricane Melissa** was a catastrophic **Category 5 storm** that struck the Caribbean, making **landfall first** **in Jamaica** before hitting Cuba. It was an extremely powerful, erratic, and catastrophic tropical cyclone
which became the **third-most intense Atlantic hurricane** on record, tied with the 1935 Labor Day hurricane, as well as the most intense at landfall in the Atlantic basin. **So, pair I is not correctly matched.**
● **Cyclone Montha** was a severe cyclonic storm that formed in the **Bay of Bengal** and made **landfall in** **India.** The name "Montha" was contributed by Thailand, meaning "fragrant flower" or **"beautiful** **flower" in Thai.** **So, pair II is not correctly matched.**
● **Typhoon Kalmaegi** was a tropical cyclone that made landfall in both the **Philippines** and **Vietnam**, causing widespread damage and casualties. The storm, which reached **Category 3-equivalent strength,** also affected Laos, Cambodia, and Thailand as it moved inland. **So, pair III is correctly matched.**
● Tropical storm **Fung-wong** made landfall in Aurora province on **Luzon island of Philippines**, as a **super** **typhoon.** The Japan Meteorological Agency (JMA) classified the system as a typhoon, and early the next day, the Philippine Atmospheric, Geophysical and Astronomical Services Administration **(PAGASA)** upgraded it to a super typhoon. **So, pair IV is correctly matched.**
**Knowledge Box**
**1. Regional Names for Tropical Storms in Various Ocean Basins:**
● North Atlantic Ocean, Caribbean Sea, Gulf of Mexico, Eastern & Central North Pacific Ocean -> Hurricanes.
● Western North Pacific Ocean & South China Sea -> Typhoons
● North Indian Ocean (Bay of Bengal and Arabian Sea) & South Indian Ocean -> Cyclones (or Tropical Cyclones)
● North-western Australia -> Willy-willies.
**2. Conditions Required for the Formation of Tropical Cyclones:**
● **Warm sea surface temperature (≥ 27°C)**: provides the latent heat of condensation,
which is the primary energy source.
● **Coriolis force**: necessary for cyclonic circulation; hence cyclones do not form near the equator (within 5° latitude).
● **Pre-existing low-pressure area**: acts as a seed disturbance for cyclone development.
● **High humidity in the lower and middle troposphere**: supports cloud formation and storm intensification.
● **Low vertical wind shear**: ensures organized circulation; strong shear disrupts cyclone structure.
● **Sufficient distance from the equator (5°–20° latitude)**: allows Coriolis force to deflect winds and initiate rotation.
**3. Naming of Tropical Cyclones:**
● Names are decided by the **WMO–ESCAP Panel on Tropical Cyclones (PTC)**, established in **1972**.
● **Member countries** of the North Indian Ocean region (13 nations, including India, Thailand, Bangladesh, Oman, etc.) **submit lists of names** in advance.
● Names are **short (≤ 8 letters), gender-neutral, culturally neutral, and easy to** **pronounce.**
● **Used sequentially** from the list as cyclones form; once used, they are **retired** if the cyclone causes severe destruction.
● **Purpose:** Simplifies communication, ensures clarity in warnings, and helps raise public awareness.
**Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Cyclone Montha, a severe cyclonic storm that formed in the Bay of Bengal, made landfall on the coast of Andhra Pradesh, India, on October 28, 2025.

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.microTopic: Reports and Indices (HDI, CPI, GHI, WEO, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to
QS World University Rankings 2026:
I. - India has emerged as the fifth most represented country in the rankings.
II.	 Indian Institute of Technology (IIT) Delhi is
India's best ranked institution.
III.	 India accounts for the highest number of new
institutional entrants in the 2026 rankings among all countries.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● India’s higher education institutions have achieved their best-ever performance in the **QS World** **University Rankings 2026**. A total of **54 universities are featured** this year, including 12 Indian Institutes of Technology (IITs).
● According to the QS World University Rankings 2026, India is now the **fourth most represented country** in the rankings, behind the **United States (192)**, the **United Kingdom (90)**, and **China (72)**. This marks a major milestone in India’s global academic footprint. **So, statement I is not correct.**
● India’s rise from **11 ranked universities in 2015** to **54 in 2026** represents an almost five-fold increase. This makes India the fastest-rising G20 nation in the rankings. The **Indian Institute of Technology (IIT)** **Delhi leads the country’s institutions, securing 123rd place globally.** Following IIT Delhi are IIT Bombay at 129th, IIT Madras at 180th, and IIT Kharagpur at 215th place. Other top institutions include the Indian Institute of Science (IISc) Bangalore at 219th and IIT Kanpur at 222nd, with the University of Delhi also making the top 350. **So, statement II is correct.**
● India has **eight new entrants,** the **highest number of new entrants** from any country in the 2026 cycle. This reflects expanding participation by both **public and private universities.** **So, statement III is** **correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**Other Key Insights from the QS World University Rankings 2026**
● **48 percent** of India’s ranked universities **improved their positions** compared to the previous year.
● **Six Indian institutions** feature in the global **top 250.** IIT Delhi leads the Indian contingent. It is ranked 123rd globally, rising from 150th in 2025.
● A total of **12 Indian Institutes of Technology (IITs)** feature in the list, highlighting their strong presence in global academia.
● **Five Indian institutions** feature in the global **top 100 for Employer Reputation.** This reflects strong industry confidence in Indian graduates.
● **Eight Indian universities** rank among the world’s **top 100 for Citations per** **Faculty.** Their average score of 43.7 is higher than that of **Germany**, the **United** **Kingdom** and the **United States**. **About QS World University Rankings**
● It is published **annually** by global higher education consultancy **Quacquarelli** **Symonds.**
● The QS rankings evaluate universities on **several parameters**, including academic reputation, **overall score**, **employer reputation**, and employment outcomes.
● This year’s rankings introduced a **new International Student Diversity (ISD)** metric as an unweighted indicator.
**Relevance:** The QS World University Rankings recently released its latest edition of rankings of higher education institutions across the world.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Industry (Waste Materials, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to the Indian Farmers Fertiliser Cooperative Limited (IFFCO):
I. - It is a multi-state cooperative engaged in both the production and marketing of fertilisers.
II. - It is not permitted to enter into joint ventures outside India for sourcing raw materials required for fertiliser production.
III. 	It was ranked as the world’s number-one
cooperative based on the ratio of turnover to GDP per capita.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Indian Farmers Fertiliser Cooperative Limited (IFFCO)** was established in **1967** as a **multi-state** **cooperative** with the objective of ensuring timely availability of **quality fertilisers** to India’s farmers. IFFCO is engaged in the **production as well as the marketing** of chemical fertilisers. The organisation is one of India’s largest manufacturers and marketers of fertilisers and has expanded its reach through an **extensive cooperative network** and commercial operations. **So, statement I is correct.**
● IFFCO **can enter into joint ventures** outside India for **sourcing raw materials** for the production of phosphatic fertilisers at economical cost. It has already established joint ventures and agreements in countries like **Jordan, Australia, Canada, and Egypt** for raw materials such as rock phosphate and potash. **So, statement II is not correct.**
● IFFCO has been ranked the **world’s number two cooperative** in the latest 2025 **International** **Cooperative Alliance (ICA)** report, based on the **turnover-to-GDP-per-capita ratio.** The top spot was claimed by another Indian cooperative — the Gujarat Cooperative Milk Marketing Federation Ltd (Amul). **So, statement III is not correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Gujarat Cooperative Milk Marketing Federation Ltd (Amul) and Indian Farmers Fertiliser Cooperative Ltd (IFFCO) are the top two cooperatives in the world, according to the ICA World Cooperative Monitor 2025.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Elections, Political Parties, Anti-Defection Law
questions.questionText:
With reference to the Special Intensive Revision (SIR) of electoral rolls, consider the following statements:
I. - The Representation of the People Act, 1950 provides the statutory basis for SIR.
II. - It involves completely online selfenumeration of electoral rolls.
III. 	The last SIR was done in 2002-2004.
IV. 	In the latest revision, voters are now required
to trace their name or a parent/relative’s name to the voter list from the last intensive revision.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **SIR, or Special Intensive Revision**, is a large-scale verification exercise that the ECI undertakes when it believes the routine annual “Summary Revision” is not enough to clean the voter rolls. It involves **house-** **to-house enumeration,** pre-filled forms, online submissions, and fresh verification of old voter data. **So,** **statement II is not correct.**
● The legal basis for SIR comes from **Section 21(3) of the Representation of the People Act, 1950,** which allows the Election Commission to order a ‘special revision’ of electoral rolls whenever it thinks necessary. Along with this, **Article 324** of the Constitution gives the ECI the power of superintendence, direction and control of elections. Together, these provisions form the constitutional and statutory backbone for conducting SIR. **So, statement I is correct.**
● BLOs are field-level officials who **collect forms, visit households and verify documents**. In the current SIR model, voters enrolled after a cut-off year (like 2003 in Bihar) are asked to submit birth and parentage documents. BLOs check and report, but they do **not** bear the burden of proving eligibility**.**
● SIR or similar **intensive revisions** have been carried out multiple times in India’s electoral history. It has already been done 8 times from 1951 till 2004. **The last SIR was done more than 21 years back in** **2002-2004.** The current SIR is the **9th such exercise. So, statement III is correct.**
● **In 2025 SIR, voters are now required to trace their name or a parent/relative’s name to the voter** **list** from the last intensive revision that occurred more than two decades ago. The commission has created an all-India database of these old rolls, available on its website. **So, statement IV is correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** The Election Commission has initiated a special intensive revision process of electoral rolls across India.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Governance and Policies
questions.questionText: Which one of the following statement best describes the pseudo-TORCH Syndrome?

options.a:
An autoimmune condition in which maternal
antibodies attack foetal neural tissues
options.b:
A developmental anomaly in infants caused
by acute nutritional deficiencies
options.c:
A neurodegenerative syndrome caused by
chronic antiviral treatment during infancy
options.d:
A genetic disorder that mimics features
of prenatal infections despite absence of infection

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Pseudo-TORCH Syndrome** is a rare, severe **autosomal recessive genetic disorder** that **clinically** **mimics congenital TORCH infections**, such as toxoplasmosis, rubella, cytomegalovirus and herpes, but occurs **without any actual infection.**
● Affected infants present with **symptoms like microcephaly**, intracranial calcifications, recurrent seizures, developmental delay, **hepatosplenomegaly, and thrombocytopenia.**
● The disorder is often associated with **consanguineous marriages** and shows high infant mortality; only a few children survive **beyond early childhood.**
● **Early diagnosis** helps avoid **unnecessary antiviral treatment** and enables genetic counselling for families. Recent advances, such as the use of **JAK inhibitors like Baricitinib,** have improved symptom control in some **surviving patients.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Indian researchers recently discovered a new mutation in the USP18 gene, causing Pseudo-TORCH Syndrome Type 2 (PTORCH2).

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Governance and Policies
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement I:** Gels and shampoos do not allow objects to move smoothly through them.
**Statement II:** These fluids continuously rearrange their internal structure in response to applied motion.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a:
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
and Statement II explains Statement I
options.b:
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
but Statement II does not explain Statement I
options.c:
Statement I is correct but Statement II is
incorrect
options.d:
Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is
correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Everyday materials like **gels, shampoos, creams, paints, and industrial slurries behave differently** from simple fluids like water or oil. They belong to a class called **non-Newtonian fluids**, meaning their **flow properties change** depending on how much force or movement is applied. **So, statement-I is** **correct.**
● New research from the Raman Research Institute shows that these fluids **rearrange their internal micro-** **structure** when an object moves through them. Because of this constant restructuring, **they do not allow** **smooth flow**; instead, they resist, deform, release, and reform, often producing irregular forces and even chaotic motion. **So, statement-II is correct.**
● The reason these materials are classified as **non-Newtonian** is because their internal structure **responds** **dynamically to motion**, leading to non-smooth flow. **So, both Statement-I and Statement-II are correct, and Statement-II is the correct explanation for** **Statement-I.**
**Relevance:** Researchers at India's Raman Research Institute (RRI) have developed a novel setup using a rheometer to probe the microscopic behavior of non-Newtonian fluids.

questions.subject: Environment
questions.microTopic: Major Species (Keystone, Indicator, Invasive, Endemic, etc.)
questions.questionText:
With reference to the recent Wildlife Institute of India’s study on newly described amphibians from Northeast India, consider the following statements:
I. - This is the highest number of vertebrate species described in a single publication in over a decade in India.
II. - Most of the newly recorded species have been discovered from the State of Arunachal Pradesh.
III. 	All the newly described species were recorded
from wildlife sanctuaries and national parks.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
A major **taxonomic revision** by the **Wildlife Institute of India** documented **13 new-to-science bush frog species** across **Northeast India**, marking the **highest vertebrate species** description in a single Indian publication **in a** **decade. So, statement I is correct.**
● **Of these 13 new species, six were recorded from Arunachal Pradesh,** three from Meghalaya, and one each from Assam, Mizoram, Nagaland, and Manipur. Seven species were found in the protected areas of Arunachal Pradesh, Assam, and Meghalaya, while one was recorded in a community-protected forest of Nagaland’s Khonoma village. **So, statement II is correct.**
○ The six species recorded from Arunachal Pradesh are Eaglenest bush frog, Arunachal bush frog, large bush frog, Dibang Valley bush frog, pointy-nosed bush frog, and eastern bush frog.
● The study records species from **both protected areas and non-protected** landscapes, including a community-protected forest in **Nagaland’s Khonoma village.** **So, statement III is not correct.**
● The six species recorded from Arunachal Pradesh are Eaglenest bush frog, Arunachal bush frog, large bush frog, Dibang Valley bush frog, pointy-nosed bush frog, and eastern bush frog. Two of these were registered from the Namdapha Tiger Reserve, and one each from Eaglenest Wildlife Sanctuary and Mehao Wildlife Sanctuary.
● The Narpuh bush frog, Mawsynram bush frog, and Boulenger bush frog were recorded from Meghalaya. The first was recorded in the Narpuh Wildlife Sanctuary and the second in Mawsynram, one of the world’s rainiest places, while the third was named after G.A. Boulenger, an authority on amphibians during the British rule in India.
● The Barak Valley bush frog was recorded in Assam’s Barail Wildlife Sanctuary, the Willong-Khullen bush frog from Manipur, the Lawngtlai bush frog from Mizoram’s Lawngtlai district, and the Khonoma bush frog from Nagaland’s Khonoma village. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** In a major taxonomic revision of bush frogs, the Dehradun-based Wildlife Institute of India (WII) has recorded 13 new-to-science species of amphibians from the northeastern part of the country.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: Nanotechnology
questions.questionText: In the context of the development of the periodic table, the term “Eka-aluminium” predicted by Dmitri Mendeleev was later discovered as which element?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **“Eka-aluminium”** was the name **Mendeleev** assigned to a then-unknown element he predicted would lie **just below aluminium in the periodic table**, based on periodic trends in atomic weight and chemical behaviour.
● In 1875, **French chemist Paul-Émile Lecoq de Boisbaudran** discovered a new element through spectroscopy while analysing zinc ore. He named it **gallium**. Its measured properties such as **low melting** **point, metallic lustre, density, atomic weight,** and oxide characteristics that matched Mendeleev’s predictions for eka-aluminium with remarkable precision, confirming the **accuracy and power of the** **periodic table.**
● This discovery was pivotal because it provided the **first experimental proof that Mendeleev’s periodic** **arrangement** could successfully predict the existence and properties of undiscovered elements.
● Gallium s confirmation, followed by **scandium (eka-boron)** and **germanium (eka-silicon),** cemented **Mendeleev’s periodic law** as a foundational principle of modern chemistry. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** 22 November 2025 marks the 150th anniversary of Dmitri Mendeleev, referred to as the Father of the Periodic Table.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
questions.microTopic: Judiciary
questions.questionText:
With reference to administrative tribunals in India,
consider the following statements:
I. - The original Constitution did not provide for the establishment of tribunals.
II.	 The decisions of tribunals are not subjected
to the writ jurisdiction of High Courts.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Tribunals** in India are specialized, **quasi-judicial bodies** created by law to provide quick, expert, and **cost-effective resolution** for specific disputes (like tax, admin, environment, corporate matters), acting as an **alternative to regular courts** to **reduce their heavy caseloads.**
● The **Constitution of India**, as adopted in 1950, contained **no provisions for tribunals.** These were introduced later by the **42nd Constitutional Amendment Act, 1976**, through **Articles 323A and 323B,** enabling the creation of administrative and other subject-specific tribunals. **So, statement I is correct.**
● The Supreme Court, in the landmark case of **L. Chandra Kumar v. Union of India (1997)**, held that **all** **decisions made by tribunals** are subject to scrutiny and judicial review by a Division Bench of the **respective High Court** within whose territorial jurisdiction the tribunal falls under **Articles 226 and 227.** **So, statement II is not correct.**
● Recently, a two-judge Bench of the Supreme Court of India has **declared the Tribunals Reforms Act,** **2021, unconstitutional.** The court found that the Act was an impermissible “legislative override” of its previous judgments — particularly its July 2021 verdict in the Madras Bar Association (V) case that had struck down identical provisions in an ordinance.
○ Consequently, the court directed that the principles laid down in its earlier judgments, which mandate a five-year tenure for tribunal members and invalidate the 50-year minimum age limit for advocates, will continue to govern all appointments. It also directed the Union government to establish a National Tribunals Commission – an independent body to oversee appointments and functioning of tribunals, which the court has been recommending for years – within four months. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** The Supreme Court has recently invalidated key provisions of the Tribunal Reforms Act 2021, citing violations of judicial independence.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: Biology and Biotechnology
questions.questionText:
Which one of the following is the best description
of “Precision Biotherapeutics”?

options.a:
It refers to medicines that are highly similar
copies of existing biological drugs, produced after the original product’s patent expires.
options.b:
It refers to medical interventions that are
designed and optimised based on a patient’s unique genetic, molecular, or cellular profile to correct the cause of disease.
options.c:
It refers to a system that focuses on the
monitoring, detection, and prevention of harmful effects of medicines after they enter public use.
options.d:
It refers to a field that deals with the computer-
based storage, analysis, and interpretation of large biological data sets.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Precision biotherapeutics** refers to a new class of medical interventions that are designed by integrating **genetic science, molecular biology and data analytics** to correct the actual cause of disease rather than merely managing symptoms.
● Unlike conventional drugs that follow a one-size-fits-all approach, these therapies are **customised** **according to an individual’s genetic, molecular and cellular profile.**
● The field operates at the **intersection of genomics, proteomics, gene editing,** **RNA-based therapeutics,** **monoclonal antibodies and artificial intelligence-driven drug discovery.**
● Technologies such as CRISPR enable direct correction of faulty genes, mRNA therapies instruct cells to synthesize or suppress specific proteins, and monoclonal antibodies precisely target cancer cells or viral proteins, while AI tools accelerate the identification of effective drug molecules. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**Key Facts About Precision Biotherapeutics:**
● The field draws on **multiple cutting-edge technologies:** ○ **Genomic and proteomic analysis**: decoding a person’s genetic and protein signatures to identify mutations or dysfunctions causing disease. ○ **Gene editing therapies:** directly modifying genes to correct underlying problems (e.g., CRISPR-based treatments for blood disorders). ○ **mRNA and nucleic acid therapeutics:** using RNA molecules to instruct cells to produce specific proteins or suppress harmful ones. ○ **Monoclonal antibodies and biologics:** laboratory-engineered molecules that bind to precise disease targets, such as cancer cells or viral proteins. ○ **AI-driven drug discovery:** leveraging big data and machine learning to predict how molecules interact within the body.
● **The Department of Biotechnology** and its funding agency **Biotechnology** **Industry Research Assistance Council,** have included it among the six focus areas of the **Biotechnology for Economy, Environment, and** **Employment Policy for** strengthening biotechnology for economic growth, environmental sustainability and employment generation.
● **Research institutions** such as the Institute of Genomics and Integrative Biology, the National Institute of Biomedical Genomics and the Translational Health Science and Technology Institute are actively mapping genetic diversity and disease susceptibility.
● In the **private sector**, companies like Biocon Biologics and Dr. Reddy’s Laboratories are advancing biosimilars and monoclonal antibodies, while Zydus LifeSciences is working on gene therapies for rare diseases.
● Globally, the United States and the European Union dominate leadership in precision medicine, while China, Japan, Singapore and South Korea are advancing rapidly.
**Relevance:** The global precision medicine market is set to surpass $22 billion by 2027, with India positioning itself as a hub.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: Space
questions.questionText:
With reference to the Coronal Mass Ejection,
consider the following statements :
I. - It involves the large-scale ejection of magnetised plasma from the Sun’s corona into interplanetary space.
II.	 It propagates at speeds comparable to or
faster than electromagnetic radiation.
III.	 It can also be observed on stars other than the
Sun.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Coronal Mass Ejections (CMEs)** are massive eruptions of **magnetised plasma** from the **Sun’s outer atmosphere** **(corona)** into **interplanetary space**. **So, statement I is correct.**
● They originate due to **magnetic reconnection**, an explosive reconfiguration of the Sun’s magnetic field, though their exact formation mechanism is still not fully understood.
● The first recorded solar storm occurred in 1859 and **it reached** **Earth in about 17 hours,** whereas light takes only about **8** **minutes** to travel from the Sun to the Earth. **So, statement II is** **not correct.**
● CMEs directed toward Earth appear as “halo CMEs” in coronagraph images. Fast CMEs generate interplanetary shock waves in the solar wind and are the main drivers of intense geomagnetic storms.
● Recently, astronomers have, for the **first time**, detected a **coronal mass ejection (CME)** on a star **other** **than the Sun**, marking a major breakthrough in **stellar space weather studies**. **So, statement III is** **correct.**
○ The discovery was made using data from the **European radio telescope network LOFAR** and has been published in the journal Nature. ○ The CME originated from a red dwarf star named **StKM 1-1262**, located over 133 light-years away. The explosion lasted only about one minute, but was found to be at least 10,000 times more violent than the most powerful solar storms ever recorded on the Sun. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Recently, a coronal mass ejection on a distant red dwarf was found to be at least 10,000 times more violent than known storms on the Sun.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: Information and Communication Technology, Robotics, AI
questions.questionText:
With reference to UNESCO s recommendations
on the ethics of neurotechnology, consider the following statements:
I. - It is the first global normative framework on the ethics of neurotechnology.
II.	 It explicitly prohibits any use of neural or
non-neural data for manipulative or deceptive purposes.
III. It specifically addresses the protection of
vulnerable populations such as children and older adults.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Neurotechnology** refers to devices and procedures that access, assess, and act on neural systems, including the human brain. If the brain were a radio station, neurotechnology is the set of devices to help to tune in.
● Neurotechnology allows neurodata (a.k.a. neural or brain data) to be decoded, giving rise to concerns about user privacy, protection against misuse, and informed consent among users.
● Broadly, neurotechnology merges advances in neuroscience, engineering, and advanced computing to evolve solutions that improve brain function and enhance human capabilities and it has made rapid strides.
● **The United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization (UNESCO)** issued the **first** **global normative framework on the ethics of neurotechnology** on November 5, which is set to enter into force on November 12. **So, statement I is correct.**
● The recommendations are based on a three-pronged strategy: ○ Defining the nature and scope of neurotechnology and neurodata. So, statement II is correct.
○ Identifying the values, principles and offering directions to nations to incorporate the recommendations with a focus on particular sectors (health and education, among others); ○ **and considerations for vulnerable populations such as children and older adults. So,** **statement II is correct.**
● The recommendations advocate for an open science model, ensuring that research outcomes are freely accessible to everyone. Open science models work like a public library: the data, software, technology, and methods are to be shared openly so that anyone can verify, reuse, and/or build on it.
● In advancing these principles, the recommendations **explicitly prohibit any use of neural or non-neural** **data for manipulative or deceptive purposes**, including in political, medical and commercial contexts. They also heighten attention towards the principles of autonomy, free will, and informed consent in any valid uses of neurotechnology. **So, statement III is correct.**
● These are the result of extensive consultations since 2021, and the framework focuses on human dignity, human rights, gender equality, social and global justice, and sustainable development, and recognises the vast potential of neurotechnology innovation for medical and assistive applications. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Recently, UNESCO issued the first global normative framework on the ethics of neurotechnology.

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.microTopic: India and Neighbourhood (Pakistan, China, Nepal, Sri Lanka, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard
to the Comprehensive Nuclear-Test-Ban Treaty (CTBT):
I. - It permits nuclear test explosions conducted for civilian purposes under international supervision.
II.	 It cannot enter into force unless all 44
Annex-2 nuclear-capable States ratify it.
III.	 India is a non-ratifying signatory country to
the treaty.
IV.	 Only North Korea has conducted nuclear test
explosions after the CTBT was opened for signature.
Which of the statements given above is/are **not** correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Comprehensive Nuclear-Test-Ban Treaty (CTBT)** bans all nuclear test explosions, whether for military or civilian purposes. **So, statement I is not correct.**
● The Treaty was opened for signature in 1996 to stop the development of new nuclear weapons and prevent upgrades to existing ones, supporting global efforts in non-proliferation and disarmament.
● CTBT enforcement requires ratification by **44 Annex 2 nuclear-capable states**. Although 187 states have signed and 178 have ratified the treaty, it's not yet in force legally. The **treaty cannot formally enter into** **force until it is ratified by 44 specific nations**, nine of which must still do so: China, **India**, Pakistan, North Korea, Israel, Iran, Egypt, Russia and the United States. **So, statements II and III are correct.**
● Since the CTBT came up in September 1996, only three countries have tested nukes — India and Pakistan in 1998, and North Korea from 2006 to 2017. **So, statement IV is not correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge box**
**International Monitoring System (IMS)**:
● It is the **core verification pillar** of the **Comprehensive Nuclear-Test-Ban Treaty** **(CTBT)** regime and forms the **technical backbone** of the work of the Comprehensive Nuclear-Test-Ban Treaty Organization.
● It was established by the **CTBTO Preparatory Commission** to ensure **global,** **continuous detection of nuclear explosions** even before the treaty formally enters into force.
● The IMS comprises 337 monitoring facilities worldwide that operate using four complementary technologies: **seismic**, **hydroacoustic**, **infrasound**, and
**radionuclide detection**. **Seismic stations** detect underground nuclear tests by recording shock waves through the Earth’s crust.
**Relevance:** Recently, U.S. President Donald Trump’s threat to restart nuclear testing poses immediate and longerterm risks for the global test ban.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: Biology and Biotechnology
questions.questionText:
Which one of the following countries is situated
completely south of the Equator?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Equator is the imaginary line that divides the Earth into the Northern and Southern Hemispheres.
● **Tanzania** is an East African country situated **just south of the Equator.** It was formed out of the union of two sovereign states namely Tanganyika and Zanzibar.
○ **Dodoma,** since 1974, the designated official capital of Tanzania, is centrally located on the mainland. **Dar es Salaam** is the largest city and port in the country. ○ The Equator crosses northern Tanzania, particularly through the Kagera Region near **Lake** **Victoria**.
● **Kenya** is one of only 13 countries in the world crossed by the Equator, the imaginary line that divides the Earth into the Northern and Southern Hemispheres. Its northernmost and southernmost points are approximately equidistant--a little over 40 north and south of the equator.
● **Mauritania** is a country on the Atlantic coast of Africa. It lies far north of the Equator and is crossed by the Tropic of Cancer, placing it in the dry tropical zone.
○ Mauritania forms a geographic and cultural bridge between the North African Maghrib (a region that also includes Morocco, Algeria, and Tunisia) and the westernmost portion of sub-Saharan Africa.
● **Chad** is a landlocked country in north-central Africa. It does **not touch the Equator and lies entirely in** **the Northern Hemisphere,** with the southernmost point is around 7°N latitude.
**Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Samia Suluhu Hassan was recently sworn in as the President of Tanzania amid violent protests and an internet blackout.

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.microTopic: Places in News and Geopolitical Hotspots (Ukraine, Taiwan Strait, Hormuz, etc.)
questions.questionText: Missing Q36
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Interstellar objects** are leftover fragments (comets and asteroids) of the process of the birth of a solar system that are ejected out of their home star systems and whose path takes them through our Solar System.
● These are important for science because it gives us the opportunity to study a sample from another solar system “up close” in a way that would not otherwise be possible.
● **Interstellar Comet 3I/ATLAS** was discovered on **July 1, 2025** by the Asteroid Terrestrial-impact Last Alert System. It is only the **third known interstellar object** to enter our Solar System after 1I/‘Oumuamua and 2I/Borisov.
● It follows a **hyperbolic trajectory**, confirming its **extrasolar** **origin**, and is estimated to be **over seven billion years old**, making it possibly the **oldest comet ever observed**.
● A major breakthrough came when the Neil Gehrels Swift Observatory detected a **faint ultraviolet signature of hydroxyl** **(OH) gas**, which forms when **sunlight breaks apart water** **molecules**. This provided the **first chemical evidence of water activity on an interstellar comet** at such a large distance from the Sun.
● The comet was observed releasing about **40 kg of water per second**, despite being located far beyond the normal **ice-vaporisation zone**, indicating **unknown physical processes** at work.
● Scientifically, 3I/ATLAS is crucial because it shows that **planetary systems beyond the Sun may** **contain water and similar chemical building blocks**.
● Unlike **‘Oumuamua, which was dry**, and **Borisov, which was carbon-monoxide rich**, 3I/ATLAS reveals **water-rich chemistry**, highlighting the **diversity of interstellar objects**.
● From an **astrobiological perspective**, this strengthens the idea that **the ingredients of life’s chemistry** **are widespread across the Galaxy**, though it does not confirm extraterrestrial life. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Recently, scientists confirmed the first chemical evidence of water on an interstellar comet (3I/ATLAS).

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.microTopic: Global Groupings and Agreements (FTA, Paris Agreement, NPT, QUAD, etc.)
questions.questionText: Missing Q37
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Digital Sequence Information (DSI)** is a placeholder term used in international policy fora that refer to data derived from dematerialized genetic resources (GR).
● The Conference of the Parties (COP) to the **Convention on Biological Diversity** and the Conference of the Parties serving as the meeting of the Parties to the Nagoya Protocol **first addressed the issue of digital** **sequence information (DSI) on genetic resources** at their respective meetings held in December 2016.
● In December 2022, at its fifteenth meeting, the COP decided to establish a multilateral mechanism for benefit-sharing from the use of DSI on genetic resources. Digital sequence information on genetic resources was also included in Goal C and Target 13 of the Kunming-Montreal Global Biodiversity Framework.
● At its sixteenth meeting, COP, with decision 16/2 adopted the modalities for operationalizing the multilateral mechanism for the fair and equitable sharing of benefits from the use of DSI on genetic resources, including a global fund, as set out in the annex to the decision. The COP decided that the global fund will be known as the "Cali Fund for the Fair and Equitable Sharing of Benefits from the Use of Digital Sequence Information on Genetic Resources". **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Recently, countries gathered in Lima for the ITPGRFA meeting face rising concern over Digital Sequence Information (DSI).

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.microTopic: Places in News and Geopolitical Hotspots (Ukraine, Taiwan Strait, Hormuz, etc.)
questions.questionText: Missing Q38
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **ozone hole** refers to the **severe seasonal** **depletion of stratospheric ozone over Antarctica**, occurring mainly during the **polar** **spring** **(September–October)**.
● The **ozone layer**, located in the stratosphere at altitudes of 9–22 miles, protects Earth from **harmful** **ultraviolet-B (UV-B) radiation**, which causes **skin** **cancer**, damages **marine ecosystems**, and reduces **plant productivity**.
● Although **ozone-depleting substances (ODS)** are present throughout the stratosphere globally, the ozone hole forms specifically over Antarctica due to **unique polar meteorological and chemical** **conditions**.
● Antarctic total ozone values in early spring are systematically lower than those in the Arctic early spring.
● There is a **significant seasonal depletion of the Arctic ozone layer** that now occurs in **most years during** **late winter and early spring (January–March)**. However, this depletion is **less severe and less** **persistent** than the Antarctic ozone hole. **So, statement I is correct.**
● The **Antarctic stratosphere** is characterised by a **stable polar vortex**, **extremely cold temperatures**, and **long-lasting polar stratospheric clouds (PSCs)**, which together favour **sustained and severe ozone** **destruction**. **So, statement II is correct.**
● In contrast, **ozone depletion in the Arctic is limited** because **average stratospheric temperatures are** **significantly higher** and the **isolation of polar air is weaker**. This is due to the greater presence of landmasses, mountains, and contrasting ocean–land regions in the Northern Hemisphere, which generate **frequent meteorological disturbances** that **warm the Arctic stratosphere**.
● The **Northern Hemisphere polar vortex is weaker and more disturbed**, allowing ozone-rich air from mid-latitudes to mix into the polar region. This dilutes ozone loss and interrupts chemical destruction, unlike in Antarctica, where the **strong, stable vortex isolates air for months**, enabling **extreme ozone** **depletion**.
● Although the chemical mechanism of ozone depletion is the same in both regions involving reactive halogen gases, especially chlorine monoxide (ClO), formed on PSCs, the shorter duration and lower frequency of PSC formation in the Arctic (due to warmer and variable temperatures) prevent long-term chlorine activation and large-scale ozone loss. **So, both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II explains Statement I**
**Relevance:** The NOAA–NASA analysis confirms that the Montreal Protocol and its amendments are effectively driving the gradual recovery of the ozone layer.

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.microTopic: India and the World (USA, Russia, Middle East, Africa, etc.)
questions.questionText: Missing Q39
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Representation of the People Act (RP), 1951,** particularly Sections 33 to 36, and the Conduct of Elections Rules, 1961, **govern the nomination process.** Section 33 prescribes who may file nominations; Section 34 provides for security deposits; and Section 36 authorises the RO to scrutinise nominations and reject those deemed invalid.
● One of the most frequent grounds of rejection is the **oath-related technicality**. Every candidate must take the **mandatory oath** after filing the nomination but **before scrutiny**, and it must be taken **before a** **specified authority**. If the oath is taken **too early**, **too late**, or before the **wrong authority**, the nomination is rejected irrespective of the candidate’s eligibility. **So, point I is correct.**
● Another common rejection ground is the **security deposit procedure**, often called the **treasury trap**. The deposit must be made within the prescribed time and only through **approved modes such as cash or** **treasury challan**. Even if a candidate has the **correct amount**, arriving after **3 PM** or using an **incorrect** **payment mode** can lead to outright rejection.  **So, point II is correct.**
● The **notarisation requirement** is also a major technical barrier. The **Form 26 affidavit**, which contains details of **assets, liabilities and criminal cases**, must be **properly notarised by a designated authority**. Any defect in notarisation, even if the information is otherwise correct, can result in rejection.
● **The nomination of a candidate cannot be rejected for suppressing or giving false information in the** **affidavit**. The copies of the nomination papers filed by each candidate along with a copy of the affidavit
accompanying the nomination should be displayed on the notice board in the office of RO on the day the nomination is filed.
○ If anyone furnishes any information contradicting the statements in the nomination form or affidavits by means of a duly sworn affidavit, copies of such affidavits should also be displayed on the notice board. ○ If the RO is satisfied that the information given by the candidate in the affidavit is wrong he is required to file a formal complaint before the appropriate Court under section 125A of the R.P. Act, 1951 and Section 177 of IPC (read with section 200 Cr PC). **So, point III is not correct.**
● Many candidates are disqualified due to delays or defects in the **submission of mandatory certificates**. These include **no-dues certificates** from municipal bodies, electricity departments or government offices, as well as **clearance certificates for government servants** from the Election Commission. Any **delay,** **missing document, or administrative hold-up** at these issuing offices becomes a **chokepoint** capable of eliminating a candidate.
● Minor clerical and procedural errors are also grounds for rejection. These include **missing signatures**, **mismatched electoral roll numbers**, **blank columns in affidavits**, **misspelt names**, and even **late** **submission by a few minutes**, such as filing at **3:05 PM instead of 3:00 PM**. **So, point IV is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Recent municipal council elections in Dadra and Nagar Haveli saw multiple nomination rejections, including without hearings, leading to unopposed victories in several wards.

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.microTopic: Global Groupings and Agreements (FTA, Paris Agreement, NPT, QUAD, etc.)
questions.questionText: Missing Q40
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **SWIFT** (Society for Worldwide Interbank Financial Telecommunication) is a **global provider of secure** **financial messaging services**, founded in **1973** to replace the **telex system**.
● It is a **member-owned cooperative** connecting over **11,000 banks, financial institutions and** **corporations** across **200+ countries and territories**.
● SWIFT is governed under **Belgian law** and is owned by about **2,400 shareholder financial institutions**. These shareholders elect a **Board of 25 Directors**, while day-to-day operations are managed by the **Executive Committee led by the CEO**.
● It is **overseen by the G-10 ((Group of Ten) central banks (not controlled and owned)**, along with the **European Central Bank**, with the **National Bank of Belgium** acting as the **lead overseer**. **So, statement** **I is correct and II is not correct.**
● SWIFT is **headquartered in Belgium** and operates through **26 international offices**. It functions purely as a **messaging platform** and does **not transfer money directly**. **So, statement III is not correct.**
● SWIFT is **neutral** and does **not monitor transaction content**. Responsibility for **sanctions compliance** lies with the **financial institutions and national regulators**, while SWIFT only provides **technical tools** such as **sanctions screening, sanctions testing**, and enhanced **payment message formats** aligned with **FATF standards**.
● The **SWIFT Oversight Forum**, established in 2012, expanded supervision beyond the G-10 to include major central banks, such as the Reserve Bank of India, the People’s Bank of China, the Bank of Russia, and the Monetary Authority of Singapore, among others. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: Biology and Biotechnology
questions.questionText: Missing Q41
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Ecuador** holds a unique geographical distinction of being the **closest country to outer space**, not because of altitude alone but due to the **shape of the Earth**.
● The Earth is not a perfect sphere; it is an **oblate spheroid**, meaning it is **flattened at the poles** and **bulges at the** **equator**.
● Because of this, locations near the **equator** are actually **farther from the Earth’s centre**, making them **closer to** **space** compared to locations at higher latitudes, even if those places have taller mountains above sea level.
● The **Kármán line** marks the official boundary of **space** at about **100 km above Earth**, but true closeness to space is measured from the **Earth’s centre**, not from **sea level**, making **geographical location** crucial.
● Due to the **equatorial bulge**, the summit of **Mount** **Chimborazo** in **Ecuador**, although lower in height than Mount Everest, is actually **farther from the Earth’s** **centre**, making it the **closest point on Earth to outer space**.
● A person standing on Chimborazo is about **2,000 metres closer to space** than one standing on Everest.
● **Ecuador** is a **northwestern South American country**. It is situated on the Equator, from which its name derives, it borders Colombia to the north, Peru to the east and the south, and the Pacific Ocean to the west. It includes the Pacific archipelago of the Galapagos Islands (Archipiélago de Colón). It is known for its exceptional **environmental diversity** and major contributions to the **environmental sciences**.
● The **first scientific expedition to measure Earth’s circumference**, led by Charles-Marie de La Condamine, was conducted here. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Recently, Ecuadoreans have voted against allowing the return of foreign military bases in the country.

questions.subject: Environment
questions.microTopic: Greenhouse Gases (GHG), ODS, Related Terms, Global Warming
questions.questionText: Missing Q42
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The term **“methane bomb”** refers to a projected **climate feedback mechanism** in the **Arctic permafrost** **region**, where warming temperatures thaw long-frozen soils.
● As permafrost melts, **stored organic carbon** decomposes, releasing large quantities of **methane (CH₄),** a greenhouse gas **far more potent than CO₂** in the short term, potentially accelerating global warming.
● Traditionally it was feared that thawing Arctic soils would **unleash vast methane pulses,** triggering runaway climate change.
● However, recent studies show that **methane-eating microbes (methanotrophs**) may outcompete **methane-producing microbes (methanogens)** in dry thaw conditions, partially offsetting emissions.
● Thus, while permafrost thaw remains a **strong climate concern,** the new evidence suggests the methane bomb scenario **may not be as inevitable or catastrophic** as once thought. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
● It s proposal introduces a **100-point rating system** across **five pillars:** Crash Protection, Vulnerable Road-User Protection, Safe Driving, Accident Avoidance, and Post-Crash Safety. **Each pillar carries a** **specific weightage** (e.g., Crash Protection 55%, VRU Protection 20%, Safe Driving 10%, Accident Avoidance 10%, Post-Crash 5%). **So, statement I is correct.**
● **Manufacturers may choose to submit** models for testing to obtain star ratings, which they can then use for **consumer information** and marketing.  There is **no blanket legal requirement** that every manufacturer or every model sold in India must be tested under Bharat NCAP. **So, statement II is not** **correct.**
● Under Bharat NCAP 2.0 **Electronic Stability Control (ESC)** and **curtain airbags** are proposed as **compulsory** for any model seeking a star rating at all.  **Autonomous Emergency Braking (AEB/AEBS)** is explicitly stated as **optional**; it is encouraged and may improve scores, especially under **Accident** **Avoidance** and **VRU Protection** verticals, but it is not a mandatory prerequisite even for a **5-star rating**. **So, statement III is not correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** The Ministry of Road Transport and Highways (MoRTH) recently released a comprehensive draft for Bharat NCAP 2.0.

With reference to Belem Health Action Plan,
consider the following statements:
I. - It was unveiled at the 2025 Global Conference on Climate and Health in Brasília.

for climate-related health interventions.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
• The **Belem Health Action Plan**, a flagship outcome of Brazil’s COP 30 Presidency, was unveiled on the **dedicated Health Day of COP30** on 13 November 2025. It is aimed at strengthening global health systems to cope better with the impacts of climate change. **So, statement I is not correct.**
