questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IPR, Institutions and Policies for IPR
questions.questionText:
Which one of the following best describes the
auditory fusion?

options.a:
The ability of the brain to ignore background
noise when focusing on speech
options.b:
The loss of pitch distinction when two tones
share the same frequency
options.c:
The phenomenon in which two closely timed
sounds are perceived as one single sound
options.d:
A hearing defect caused by damage to the
inner ear hair cells

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Auditory fusion** is the **brain’s tendency** to treat **two sounds** arriving within a very **short interval** as one **unified event.**
● The **minimum time gap** required to hear the sounds separately is called the **fusion threshold**, typically around **2–3 milliseconds** for brief clicks and up to 5–10 milliseconds or more for **complex tones or** **syllables.**
● It **helps everyday listening** by preventing echoes from becoming confusing clusters; instead, the brain fuses the first sound and its **early reflections** into a single percept.
● It is **distinct from masking**, which occurs when **one sound hides** another rather than merging it. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Auditory fusion plays a crucial role in several aspects of sound perception and communication by integrating various acoustic cues into a single, coherent experience.

questions.subject: Environment
questions.microTopic: Air and Water Acts
questions.questionText:
With reference to Corporate Average Fuel
Efficiency (CAFE) norms in India, consider the following statements:
I. - They aim to regulate CO₂ emissions from passenger vehicles under 3,500 kg.
II.	 They apply to vehicles powered by petrol,
diesel and LPG only.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Corporate Average Fuel Efficiency (CAFE)** norms are government-mandated standards that require **auto manufacturers** to meet a fleet-wide **average fuel economy target.**
● These are introduced by the **Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE)** in 2017, and aim to regulate **fuel** **consumption** and **CO₂ emissions** from passenger vehicles **under 3,500 kg. So, statement I is correct.**
● In India, CAFE norms were **introduced in two phases,** with the first stage effective from 2017-18 and the second from 2022-23. CAFE norms were designed to **reduce oil imports,** **cut air pollution**, and promote **cleaner vehicles** like EVs, CNG cars, and hybrids.
● These norms apply to vehicles powered by **petrol, diesel, LPG, CNG, hybrids, and electric power. So,** **statement II is not correct.**
**Knowledge Box**
**About Bureau of Energy Efficiency**
● It was **established in 2002** under the provisions of **the Energy Conservation Act,** **2001.**
● The primary objective of BEE is to **reduce energy intensity** in the Indian economy.
● It coordinates with **designated consumers**, designated agencies and **other** **organizations;** recognizes, identifies and utilizes the existing resources and infrastructure in **performing the functions** assigned to it under **the Energy** **Conservation Act.**
**Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** India has proposed to significantly revamp its key vehicle emissions rules, called the Corporate Average Fuel Efficiency (CAFE) norms.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IPR, Institutions and Policies for IPR
questions.questionText:
Sinapic acid which was recently in the news, is
being explored primarily for its potential use in
which of the following?

options.a:
As a plant hormone regulating seed
germination
options.b:
As a natural antioxidant compound that
accelerates wound healing in diabetic patients
options.c:
As a preservative to increase shelf life of
processed dairy products
options.d:
As an organic pesticide used in integrated
pest-management (IPM) systems

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Sinapic acid** is a **naturally** occurring **plant-based antioxidant** found in many edible plants. Recently researchers from Nagaland University demonstrated that Sinapic acid when given orally can significantly **accelerate wound healing in diabetic condition**s. **Sinapic acid promotes wound healing in diabetic** **conditions** by activating the SIRT1 pathway, which plays a critical role in tissue repair, angiogenesis (formation of new blood vessels), and inflammation control. This discovery is important because diabetic patients often suffer delayed wound healing, leading to infections, chronic ulcers, and even amputations. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Nagaland University identifies natural compounds with potential for diabetic wounds treatment.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IPR, Institutions and Policies for IPR
questions.questionText:
The recycling of plastics is practically restricted
to only certain categories because:

options.a: Most plastics lose their electrostatic stability
options.b:
Majority of plastics absorb atmospheric
moisture
options.c:
Different plastics have different polymer
structures and additives
options.d:
Composite plastics generate unstable chemical
radicals when heated

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Plastics are a broad category** of materials, each with **different polymer structures, additives, and** **chemical behaviors.** These **differences determine** whether a plastic softens, melts, cracks, or decomposes under heat directly affecting **whether it can be recycled.** Thermoplastics such as PET and HDPE can soften and harden repeatedly, making them suitable for recycling, whereas thermoset plastics form permanent chemical bonds and cannot be melted again. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:**  New research suggests that global plastic packaging pollution, currently about 66 million tonnes annually, could be nearly eliminated by 2040 mainly through reuse and return systems.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.microTopic: International Organisations (WTO, IMF, World Bank, RCEP, FTAs, etc.)
questions.questionText:
With reference to the Integrated Forum on
Climate Change and Trade (IFCCT), consider the following statements:
I. - It was formally launched at COP30 to the United Nations Framework Convention on
Climate Change (UNFCC).
II.	 It will be institutionally independent of both
the World Trade Organization (WTO) and the UNFCCC.
III.	 It will adjudicate climate disputes or evaluate
specific national measures.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Recently, the **30th Conference of the Parties (COP30) to the United Nations Framework Convention** **on Climate Change** **(UNFCC)** held in Belém, Brazil in 2025 that formally launched an integrated forum on Climate Change and Trade (IFCCT). **So, statement I is correct.**
● **The IFCCT will be institutionally independent of both the World Trade Organization (WTO) and** **the UNFCCC** and will respect their mandates. It will not seek to make rules. It will produce reference materials that countries could apply as they choose without obligation. Its purpose is not to duplicate negotiations or discussions underway in other forums, but to support them by generating ideas, finding common ground and building trust and mutual understanding between governments and the individual officials who represent them. All proceedings will be non-binding, non-negotiated and non-attributable. **So, statement II is correct.**
● IFCCT does not negotiate climate rules or trade agreements. Instead, it provides a **dialogue platform** for cooperation and mutual understanding in addressing issues like carbon-border adjustments, subsidies, and supply-chain disruptions. It has **no adjudicatory, interpretative, or treaty-making powers**. It does not issue binding decisions, does not rule on disputes, and does not assess individual countries’ policies. Its mandate is purely consultative. **So, statement III is not correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:**    Recently, the 30th Conference of the Parties (COP30) to the United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change formally launched an Integrated Forum on Climate Change and Trade (IFCCT).

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: Information and Communication Technology, Robotics, AI
questions.questionText:
A passenger aircraft flying near a big city airport
suddenly gets a false terrain warning, due to
which it shows a wrong location on its navigation screen. So, the Air Traffic Control (ATC) has to guide it manually to land the aircraft safely. Later, experts found that the aircraft’s satellite navigation signals were tampered with. Which one of the following best explains what the aircraft most likely experienced?

options.a:
Global Navigation Satellite System multipath
interference

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Modern aircraft depend heavily on **Global Navigation Satellite Systems (GNSS)** which include GPS, GLONASS, Galileo, and others, for accurate **positioning, navigation, and timing**. When these signals are disturbed, aircraft may receive **wrong location data, false terrain alerts, or navigation errors**, creating serious safety challenges.
● Recently, planes flying near **Delhi** experienced a wave of such incidents due to **GNSS spoofing**, prompting an inquiry.
● **GNSS Spoofing** involves sending **counterfeit GNSS signals** to an aircraft. These false signals **mislead** **the aircraft receivers, causing incorrect aircraft position** on the display, f**alse terrain warnings,** **wrong runway alignment, and confusion in automatic braking, surveillance and communication** **systems**. Aircraft crews often need manual instructions from ATC to maintain safety. It has become common in conflict zones, e.g.: in West Asia, Eastern Russia, borders of Pakistan and Myanmar. GNSS spoofing incidents are rising globally, with up to 1,500 flights/day affected by 2024. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
● **Multipath interference** occurs when satellite signals **bounce off buildings, terrain, or** **structures**, causing delayed, slightly inaccurate signals. However, it does not involve deliberate tampering.
● Scintillation occurs when the ionosphere causes satellite signals to **flicker or fade**, especially near the equator or poles. It can degrade signal quality, but it does **not involve** **intentional tampering**.
**Relevance:** Recently, planes flying near Delhi experienced a wave of such incidents due to GNSS spoofing, prompting an inquiry.

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.microTopic: India and the World (USA, Russia, Middle East, Africa, etc.)
questions.questionText:
With reference to Ammonium Nitrate, consider
the following statements:
I. - Pure ammonium nitrate is not an explosive on
its own.
II. - It is classified as an oxidiser under the United
Nations classification of dangerous goods.
III. In India, a license under the Ammonium
Nitrate Rules, 2012 is also required for any
activity related to ammonium nitrate.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Ammonium nitrate (NH₄NO₃)** is a white, crystalline chemical used widely in **fertilisers, cold packs,** **anaesthetic gases, and especially in commercial explosives** for mining and construction. By itself, it is **not an explosive**, but it becomes highly dangerous when mixed with fuels or other contaminants.
● **Pure ammonium nitrate** is **not an explosive on its own**. It is classified as an **oxidiser (Grade 5.1) under** **the United Nations classification of dangerous good**s. If mixed with ingredients like fuel or some other contaminants, or because of some other external factors, it can be very explosive. **So, statements I and** **II are correct.**
● When mixed with substances such as **fuel oil**, ev**en a small amount (e.g., 0.5 kg)** can become **explosive** if packed in a container and initiated. Forensic experts have noted that such mixtures can be detonated using **simple detonators**, making them attractive for use in Improvised Explosive Devices (IEDs).
● Because ammonium nitrate has high misuse potential, India regulates it strictly under **Ammonium Nitrate** **Rules, 2012** (under Explosives Act, 1884) and  **Industrial Development and Regulation Act, 1951**. These rules regulate **manufacture, conversion, bagging, import, export, transport, storage,** **possession, sale, and use** of ammonium nitrate. Handling it without a **licence** is illegal. Large-scale storage in populated areas is also prohibited. **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Recently, Delhi's L-G orders police to track ammonium nitrate sales and enhance security measures after a recent deadly blast near Red Fort.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IPR, Institutions and Policies for IPR
questions.questionText:
Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR)
and Attenuated Total Reflectance-FTIR (ATR-
FTIR) are primarily conducted for:

options.a: Measuring the physical dimensions of objects
options.b:
Determining the genetic information of
samples
options.c:
Identifying  the chemical composition of
samples
options.d: Calculating the velocity of moving objects

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer**: **c**
**Explanation:**
● Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR) and Attenuated Total Reflectance-FTIR (ATR-FTIR) are **analytical techniques used to obtain the infrared absorption spectrum** of a material.
● Every substance has a unique IR “fingerprint,” allowing scientists to **identify the chemical composition,** **functional groups, and molecular structure** of unknown samples.
● ATR-FTIR is especially useful for **analysing trace residues, explosives**, paints, fibres, biological stains, and surface contaminants without extensive sample preparation. Therefore, these methods are primarily **used for chemical identification and characterisation in forensic and material analysis**. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.** **Relevance**: FTIR and ATR‑FTIR came in the news after forensic experts used them to study debris from the Red Fort blast, identifying explosives through their chemical fingerprints.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: Biology and Biotechnology
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to
the National Investigation Agency (NIA):
I. - It is a statutory body.
II. - It cannot take over the investigation of
offences without the prior permission or
consent from State Governments.
III. 	It is empowered to investigate scheduled
offences committed outside India.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **National Investigation Agency (NIA) Act of 2008,** enacted immediately after the 26/11 Mumbai terror attacks, established the NIA under the administrative control of the Ministry of Home Affairs (MHA) as a specialised, central agency capable of investigating transnational and complex terror plots. **So, statement I is correct.**
● The agency’s primary function is to investigate and prosecute scheduled offences (mentioned in the NIA Act, 2008), which are serious crimes involving national security and those under the Unlawful Activities Prevention Act (UAPA), 1967.
● The NIA is also vested with several unique powers that ensure its smooth working as a national counterterrorism agency. The agency has jurisdiction over all states and can take over the investigation of scheduled offences anywhere in India **without prior permission or consent from state governments.** This ensures operational efficiency and eliminates jurisdictional hurdles in inter-state and trans-state cases. **So, statement II is not correct.**
● The NIA (Amendment) Act, 2019, expanded the agency’s mandate and powers: ○ **Extra-territorial jurisdictions** – The NIA was now empowered to investigate scheduled offences committed outside India, provided they are directed against Indian citizens or affect Indian interests, subject to international treaties. **So, statement III is correct.** ○ **Expanded list of scheduled offences** – The scope of the agency was expanded by adding human trafficking, counterfeiting of currency, manufacture and sale of prohibited arms, and cyberterrorism to the list of scheduled offences. ○ **Designation of Special Courts** – The amendment empowered the centre and state governments to designate sessions courts as NIA special courts for the trial of scheduled offences in consultation with the Chief Justice of the High Court under which the sessions court falls. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Recently, the NIA has arrested the 7th accused involved in the Red Fort, Delhi blast.

questions.subject: Environment
questions.microTopic: Aquatic Ecosystem and Life (Aquatic Ecosystem, Maritime Zones, Corals, Mangrove, Marine Vegetation)
questions.questionText:
Which of the following can be the possible applications of Bioacoustics?
I. - Enhancing crop health and pest control
II. - Assessing ecosystem health
III. Detecting illegal logging and forest intrusions
IV. Tracking marine mammals
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer**: **a**
**Explanation:**
● Bioacoustics is the **study of how animals make, send, and hear sounds**. It uses sensors, recorders, and AI to understand their communication, behaviour, and habitats. By **analysing sound waves like mating** **calls, movement noises, or warning signals**, scientists can learn about species. Since many animals are easier to detect by sound than sight, bioacoustics is a key tool for ecology and conservation.
● **Bio-acoustic sensing** is increasingly being applied in agriculture to **enhance crop health and pest** **control.** Acoustic sensors can detect the sounds of insect infestations, enabling farmers to implement targeted interventions before significant crop damage occurs. Additionally, monitoring plant bioacoustics can provide insights into water stress levels, optimizing irrigation practices for sustainable agriculture. **So** **point I is correct.**
● **Bioacoustic data can be used to track changes in species composition, detect habitat degradation,** **and identify the presence of invasive species**. In dense tropical forests, animals like birds, frogs, and
insects are hard to spot. Bioacoustic sensors record their calls, helping scientists count populations and find rare species. **So point II is correct**.
● Modern acoustic systems can detect sounds like chainsaws, trucks, and gunshots. This helps authorities act fast against **illegal logging and forest intrusions**. **So point III is correct**.
● Marine mammals use low or **high‑frequency sounds to communicate**. **Hydrophones record these** **sounds, helping track whales, dolphins**, and others even in deep or dark waters. **So point IV is correct**. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.** **Relevance**: Researchers at the University of Copenhagen are exploring how AI with animal sounds can transform biodiversity monitoring on land and in water.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.microTopic: Education (NEP 2020, Skill Development)
questions.questionText:
With reference to the “Deemed-to-be-University”, consider the following statements:
I. - It enjoys the academic status and privileges of a full-fledged university.
II. - It is declared only by the Central Government on the advice of the University Grants Commission.
III. 	NCERT has been granted deemed-to-
be-university status under the “General Category”.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer**: **a** **Explanation**:
● A **“deemed-to-be-university”** is an institution of higher education which is not a university by enactment of Parliament/State Legislature but is **declared as having the academic status of a university** by the **Central Government** under Section III of the UGC Act, 1956. Such institutions enjoy the academic **status and privileges of a full-fledged university**, including designing their own courses, conducting exams, and awarding degrees. **So, statement I is correct.**
● Under **Section III of the UGC Act, 1956**, the power to declare an institution as a deemed-to-be-university lies **solely with the Central Government**. The UGC evaluates the institution and sends its recommendations, but the **final decision is made by the Central Government**. **So, statement II is** **correct.**
● Under UGC rules, deemed universities are of two types: ○ **General Category**: This category includes institutions that offer **multiple, well-established** **disciplines** such as science, humanities, engineering, management, or social sciences. They have a broad academic structure and long-standing programmes. ○ **De Novo category**: It is reserved for institutions that specialise in **emerging, frontier or unique** **fields** which are not commonly taught or researched in regular universities. Examples include advanced AI research, space sciences, quantum materials, nanotechnology, or specialised educational research like NCERT. ○ Recently, **NCERT** was deemed **to be university status** under the “**De Novo” category**. This is because it focuses on specialised research in teaching methods, curriculum design, and teacher training, which regular universities usually do not cover. **So statement III is not correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.** **Relevance**: Recently, the National Council of Educational Research and Training (NCERT) has been granted deemed-to-be-university status by the Indian government.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: Biology and Biotechnology
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to Ricin:
I. - It is a naturally occurring poison present in the seeds of the castor plant.
II. - Currently, there is no specific antidote available to neutralise ricin poisoning.
III. 	It loses its toxic potency at normal
atmospheric temperatures and cannot be used for large-scale attacks.
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer**: **b** **Explanation**:
● **Ricin** is a highly **toxic protein** obtained from the **seeds of the castor plant**. It inhibits protein synthesis inside human cells, which can lead to organ failure and death. Even a small amount can be fatal if inhaled, ingested, or injected. **So statement I is correct.**
● Currently, there is **no approved antidote for ricin**. Treatment involves only supportive care, like managing breathing, hydration, and organ function. Because no antidote exists, ricin is considered a highrisk toxin by global security agencies. **So statement II is correct.**
● Ricin stays **poisonous at normal temperatures**. It remains dangerous in powder, spray, or liquid form. Large-scale attacks are hard because of handling and delivery issues, but the toxin **does not lose its toxic** **potency at normal atmospheric temperatures**. **So statement III is not correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.** **Relevance**: Recently, Gujarat ATS arrested three individuals planning nationwide terror attacks using Ricin.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Governance and Policies
questions.questionText:
The 2025 amendment to the Transplantation of
Human Organs and Tissues Rules specifically
targets the donation and transplantation of which
organ?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer**: **c** **Explanation**:
● The Transplantation of Human Organs and Tissues (Amendment) Rules, 2025  **remove the mandatory** **requirement of a clinical specular microscope** for centres conducting corneal transplants. This device is expensive and often unavailable in smaller hospitals. Removing this requirement reduces infrastructure barriers and makes it easier for more centres to conduct corneal transplants.
● The parent legislation, Transplantation of Human Organs and Tissues Act, 1994 (as amended), **continues** **to prohibit commercial dealings in human organs or tissues**.
● The rules **aim to expand access to eye donation and corneal-transplant services**, especially in underserved regions. By reducing equipment requirements and making compliance easier, the Rules are meant to help smaller and rural medical centres participate more effectively in corneal transplantation. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.** **Relevance**: Recently, the Union Ministry of Health and Family Welfare has amended the Transplantation of Human Organs and Tissues (Amendment) Rules, 2025.

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.microTopic: Places in News and Geopolitical Hotspots (Ukraine, Taiwan Strait, Hormuz, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Place in the news
Reason
I. - Peru Talks going on for Preferen­ tial Trade Agreement (PTA) with India
II. - Christmas Island
Major global tech firm’s new AI data centre
III. Ethiopia Entered a pact with India for the translocation of cheetahs
IV. - Botswana Has the largest population of elephants on Earth
How many of the pairs given above are correctly
matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Peru and India have not yet signed a Preferential Trade Agreement (PTA)** or Free Trade Agreement (FTA), but both countries are in advanced stages of negotiation, aiming to finalize the pact soon to boost trade in goods, services, and critical minerals, leveraging Peru's gateway status to Latin America. **So pair** **I is correctly matched.**
○ Peru is a country in the South American continent located in the Western side touching on the Pacific Ocean. It is bordered by Chile, Ecuador, Bolivia , Colombia, and Brazil.
● **Christmas Island, an Australian territory in the Indian Ocean, has been chosen by Google to build** **a powerful AI and cloud data centre.** The location is seen as strategic for global data routing and redundancy. **So pair II is correctly matched.**
● Ethiopia continues to face humanitarian difficulties and internal conflict, keeping it in international news. It did not sign any pact with India for the translocation of cheetahs. Recently, India signed a pact with Botswana for the translocation of cheetahs. **So pair III is not correctly matched.**
○ Ethiopia is a landlocked country on the Horn of Africa. It is bounded by Eritrea to the north, Djibouti to the northeast, Somalia to the east, Kenya to the south, and South Sudan and Sudan to the west.
● According to the World Population Review, **Botswana is estimated to shelter around 130,000** **elephants.** This is nearly a third of Africa’s total elephant population and makes the southern African country the **single biggest stronghold for the species.** Its vast savannas, wetlands, and protected areas such as Chobe National Park provide some of the best remaining elephant habitats in the world. **So, pair** **IV is correctly matched.**
○ Botswana is a landlocked country in southern Africa. It is bordered by South Africa to the south and east, by Namibia to the west, and by Zimbabwe to the east **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.** **Relevance**: Google is planning to build a large artificial intelligence data centre on Australia's remote Indian Ocean outpost of Christmas Island.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: Nanotechnology
questions.questionText: What is Altermagnetism?

options.a:
A method to measure magnetic fields around
planets
options.b:
A magnetic order that mixes traits of
ferromagnets and antiferromagnets
options.c:
A way to convert magnets into superconductors
at room temperature
options.d:
A phenomenon describing how geomagnetic
poles shift over time

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer**: **b** **Explanation**:
● Altermagnetism is a new type of magnetism found in some materials. It **combines features of** **ferromagnets and antiferromagnets**. In these materials, the spins of electrons on nearby atoms point in opposite directions, so there is **no overall magnetisation**, like in antiferromagnets. But **because of the** **crystal structure**, the **energy bands split by spin**, creating spin-polarised **current**s, a property usually seen in **ferromagnets**.
● This special property makes altermagnets useful in spintronics. They can help **build faster, energy‑saving** **devices, stable memory storage**, and even parts for quantum computers, where controlling electron spin is very important. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.** **Relevance**: The recent discovery of altermagnetism represents a step forward in physicists’ understanding of the magnetic world.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: Biology and Biotechnology
questions.questionText: The term Ozempic, recently in news is related to:

options.a:
A pancreatic beta-cell regeneration therapy
for diabetes management
options.b:
A injectable formulation of semaglutide for
adults type 2 diabetes
options.c:
A monoclonal antibody for autoimmune
diseases
options.d:
A pancreatic beta-cell regeneration therapy
for diabetes management

questions.explanationMarkdown:
• **Ozempic,** a once-weekly **injectable formulation of semaglutide**, is approved in India for adults with **type 2 diabetes as the first line treatment,** adjunct to diet and exercise. Ozempic, the original GLP-1 RA (semaglutide) therapy, researched and developed by Novo Nordisk is now available in India. • Semaglutide was recently included in WHO’s Model List of Essential Medicines - highlighting the proven health benefits of this innovative treatment for people living with diabetes. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Ozempic, the original GLP-1 RA (semaglutide) therapy is now available in India.

questions.subject: Environment
questions.microTopic: Ecology and Ecosystem
questions.questionText:
Which of the following best describes the concept
'Intergenerational Equity'?

options.a:
Promoting rapid economic growth in the
present to maximise long-term welfare
options.b:
Distributing natural resource use fairly so
future generations are not disadvantaged
options.c:
Protecting cultural traditions so they remain
unchanged across future generations
options.d:
Encouraging investment in stock markets to
increase wealth for current citizens

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The idea of **Intergenerational Equity** comes from the **Brundtland Report** (1987). It explains sustainable development as using resources to **meet today’s needs without harming the ability of future** **generations to meet theirs**. Later, the Rio Declaration (1992) made this principle official, saying that d**evelopment must balance the needs of both present and future generations**.
● In India, the Supreme Court has applied this idea in important cases like Vellore Citizens  Welfare Forum v. Union of India (1996) and M.C. Mehta v. Kamal Nath (1997). The Court ruled that **natural resources** **belong to future generations as well, so the present generation must not overuse or destroy them**. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.** **Relevance**: Recently, the Supreme Court has been exploring the new dimensions of the concept of Intergenerational Equity in the endangered species conservation programme.

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.microTopic: Current Affairs Focus (Summits, Reports, Conflicts, Visits, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. - Javokhir Sindarov has become the youngest player to win the FIDE World Cup 2025.
II. Ahmedabad has been formally confirmed
as the venue of the  centenary edition of the Commonwealth Games.
III.	 Indian women’s kabaddi team has won the
Women’s Kabaddi World Cup 2025 for the first time.
IV. Deaflympics, an international multi-sport
event for deaf athletes, is organised by the International Olympic Committee (IOC).
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **The FIDE World Cup 2025** took place in Goa, India, from October 30 to November 27. **Uzbekistan’s** **Javokhir Sindarov became the youngest player to win the Chess World Cup after he beat China’s** **Wei Yi in the final’s tiebreak.** This was the first time in 25 years that India hosted the biennial event and a record Indian players were Indian. **So, statement I is correct.**
○ The FIDE World Cup is the event that offers the top three finishers direct qualification for the 2026 Candidates Tournament, which determines the challenger for the World Chess Championship.
● **Ahmedabad** has been formally confirmed as the **venue of the 2030 Commonwealth Games.** India last hosted the CWG in 2010 in Delhi. India is likely to push for the inclusion of yoga as a medal sport at the **centenary edition**. **So, statement II is correct.**
● The Indian women’s kabaddi team won the **Women’s Kabaddi World Cup, 2025** after defeating Chinese Taipei 35–28 in the final in Dhaka, Bangladesh. This was the **Indian women’s kabaddi team’s** **second World Cup crown on the trot**, having also won the inaugural edition hosted in Patna, Bihar, back in 2012. **So, statement III is not correct.**
○ This was the second edition of the Women’s Kabaddi World Cup which was held after a gap of 13 years. In the inaugural edition, the Indian women’s team defeated the Iran team.
● **Deaflympics 2025** was held in Tokyo, Japan, this year. This was the 25th Summer Deaflympics, 101 years after the inaugural edition held in Paris in 1924. India won 20 medals – nine gold, seven silver, and four bronze – at Deaflympics 2025. Indian shooters accounted for 16 medals. India finished at rank 6th.
○ The Deaflympics is an international "Olympics for Deaf people." This is an international multisport event for deaf athletes held every four years, **organised by the International Committee of** **Sports for the Deaf** (ICSD). Though, it is recognised by the **International Olympic Committee** **(IOC)**. **So, statement IV is not correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Recently, Javokhir Sindarov has become the youngest player to win the FIDE World Cup 2025.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: Defence
questions.questionText:
Consider the following information:
Missile Devel­ oped by
Specification
I. - Minuteman
III
United States
Conventional cruise missile
II. - Burevestnik Ukraine Low-flying cruise missile
III. - INVAR India Anti-tank guid­ ed missile
IV. - Air-sol
moyenne portée
France Supersonic cruise missile
In how many of the above rows is the given information correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
●  The **LGM-30G Minuteman III** is an **intercontinental ballistic missile (ICBM)** built in the **United** **States**. Debuted in the early 1970s, the Minuteman III was the first US missile to include multiple independently targetable reentry vehicles (MIRVs). It is the sole land-based element of the American nuclear triad. **So, row I is not correctly matched.**
● The **Burevestnik**, whose name means storm petrel,  is a ground-launched, **low-flying cruise missile** that can carry a nuclear warhead and is also nuclear-powered. Developed by **Russia**, it is one of six strategic weapons announced by the Russian President in a 2018 speech. NATO code-names it ‘SSC-X-9 Skyfall’. **So, row II is not correctly matched.**
● **INVAR** is an **anti-tank guided missile** (ATGM) intended for launch from tank platforms. It can be fired from T-90 tanks and has been actively used by Indian forces, known for its precise long-range strikes. The missile can destroy enemy tanks equipped with Explosive Reactive Armour. It is manufactured by **Rosoboronexport** in **Russia** and produced in **India** under license by **Bharat Dynamics Limited** (BDL). **So, row III is not correctly matched**.
● **Air-sol moyenne portée** (ASMP) is the newest iteration of the ASMP series of medium-range, **supersonic cruise missiles** capable of carrying **nuclear weapons.** Developed by **France**, it plays a vital role in France’s nuclear deterrence strategy, serving as a key element of the air-based part of its nuclear triad**. So, row IV is correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.** **Relevance**: USA President Donald Trump directs nuclear weapons testing to resume for the first time in over 30 years.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Critical and Strategic Minerals (Lithium, Cobalt, Titanium, KABIL, REEs, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to
the Neodymium:
I. - It is the second most abundant rare-earth element after Cerium.
II.	 It is completely inert towards mineral acids.
III.	 It is used in the manufacturing of optical
devices and electric vehicles.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer: c** **Explanation**:
● **Neodymium (Nd),** with an atomic number of 60, is a chemical element belonging to the lanthanide series within the rare earth elements. Discovered by **Karl Auer** in Vienna in 1885, it is the **second most** **abundant rare-earth element** after **cerium**. Neodymium occurs in minerals like **monazite** and **bastnasite**, with significant deposits found primarily in Brazil, China, the USA, India, Sri Lanka, and Australia. **So, statement I is correct.**
● Neodymium is a **ductile, malleable, silvery-white metal** that quickly oxidises in air to form Nd2O3,
which easily spalls and exposes the metal to further oxidation. To prevent degradation, it should be stored in a sealed container, in a vacuum, or in an inert atmosphere. The metal **reacts slowly with most mineral** **acids**, except hydrofluoric acid (HF), with which it forms a protective layer of NdF3**. So, statement II is** **not correct.**
● Neodymium plays a crucial role in the global rare-earth magnet industry. It is essential for **electric** **vehicles**, **clean energy technologies**, and **defence systems**. Additionally, it is used in **optical devices** and **electrical applications. So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.** **Relevance**: India is expected to experience a significant rise in neodymium production by the end of 2027.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IPR, Institutions and Policies for IPR
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to the Protection of Plant Varieties and Farmers’(PPV&FRA) Rights Authority:
I. - It is a non-statutory organisation established to grant intellectual property rights to plant breeders for their innovations.
II.	 Its members include representatives from
tribal, farmers, and women organisations nominated by the central government.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The Protection of Plant Varieties and Farmers’ Rights Authority (PPV&FRA) is a **statutory body** established under the **Protection of Plant Varieties and Farmers’ Rights Act, 2001**, under the **Ministry** **of Agriculture & Farmers’ Welfare**, Government of India. The Authority is headquartered in New Delhi. Its main objectives are to grant intellectual property rights to plant breeders for their innovations in
developing new plant varieties and to maintain the **National Register of Plant Varieties (NRPV)**, ensuring proper documentation and conservation of valuable germplasm resources**.** **So, statement I is not** **correct.**
● The Protection of Plant Varieties and Farmers’ Rights Authority (PPV&FRA) has **15 members as** **notified by the Government of India** (GOI). Eight of them are ex officio members representing various Departments/Ministries, three are from SAUs, and the remaining are from state governments. **One** **representative each is nominated by the Central Government for farmers, tribal organisations, the** **seed industry, and women's organisations involved in agricultural activities**. The Registrar General is the ex officio Member Secretary of the Authority. **So, statement II is correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.** **Relevance**: Recently, the Silver Jubilee of the PPV&FRA Act and the 21st Foundation Day of the Protection of Plant Varieties and Farmers’ Rights Authority have been celebrated.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: Defence
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to
the Indian Territorial Army:
I. - It was formally inaugurated by the first Indian Army chief General K. M. Cariappa.
II.	 It is a part-time voluntary military force that
supports and augments the regular Indian Army.
III.	 Gainful employment or self-employment in
a civil profession is a prerequisite for joining the territorial army.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer: b** **Explanation**:
● The genesis of the Territorial Army in India can be traced back to the **First War of Independence in** **1857**, when a Volunteer Force was raised. After independence, the Territorial Army Act was passed in 1948. The Territorial Army was formally inaugurated by the **first Indian Governor-General, Shri C** **Rajagopalachari**, on 09 October 1949. This day is celebrated as Territorial Army Day every year to raise awareness among the countrymen about the existence of the ‘Citizens’ Army.’ **So, statement I is not** **correct.**
● The Indian Territorial Army is a **part-time voluntary military** force that supports and augments the regular Indian Army. Also referred to as ‘Terriers’ or "**Secondary Line of Defence**," they are civilians who receive military training, enabling them to serve in uniform while maintaining their primary or civilian profession, thereby combining patriotism with practical service. **So, statement II is correct**.
● Indian Citizens aged between **18 and 42 years** who have passed the graduation exam from a recognised university are eligible to join as civilians in the TA. Other eligibility criteria include physical and mental fitness and **gainful employment** in the (**Central Govt. / State Govt. / Semi Govt. / PSU / Pvt. Sector /** **Self Employed / Own Business**) category**. So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.** **Relevance**: In a first-of-its-kind move, the Army is planning to induct women soldiers in its Territorial Army (TA) battalions.

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: GI Tags, Awards & Honours
questions.questionText: Vrindavani Vastra, recently seen in news is a:

options.a:
Palm-leaf manuscript art from Odisha,
narrating episodes from the Bhagavata Purana
options.b:
Traditional textile from Assam, woven with
the theme of Krishna’s various exploits
options.c:
Cotton scroll painting from Manipur, used in
Vaishnav devotional performances
options.d:
Ritual cloth of the Bhutia tribe of Sikkim,
known for its geometric motifs

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Recently the Assam Chief Minister travelled to London to initiate the process of bringing **Vrindavani** **Vastra**  back from the British museum. It is a rare **400-year-old Assamese textile**, considered a masterpiece of sacred **Vaishnava art**.
● The Vrindavani Vastra is a **traditional silk textile from Assam**, woven 400 years ago under the guidance of S**rimanta Sankardev**a at the request of K**och king Nara Narayan.** It depicts K**rishna’s childhood** **exploits in Vrindavan**, showing scenes of his divine play with gopas, gopis, and animals.
● This textile was woven using the **complex lampas technique** that required two weavers working simultaneously and used multicoloured threads such as red, black, yellow, green, white, etc.
● Over time, parts of the Vastra travelled from **Assam to Tibet** and were eventually collected by British explorers. The B**ritish Museum acquired it in 1904** and currently preserves a nine-and-a-half-metre assemblage of its surviving pieces. It stands as a testament to Assamese weaving traditions and Vaishnava cultural heritage. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Chief Minister Himanta Biswa Sarma left for London to initiate the process for the revered silk textile linked to 15th-16th century saint-reformer Srimanta Sankaradeva

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Interior of Earth and Continental Theories
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to
the Siliguri Corridor:
I. - It is wedged between Nepal and Bhutan to the north and Bangladesh to the south.
II.	 The transboundary rivers Mahananda and
flows through this corridor.
III.	 The Tri-Shakti Corps has been in-charge of
securing the corridor.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Siliguri Corridor** often called India's ‘Chicken’s Neck’ is a narrow but extremely strategic strip of land in **northern West Bengal** that links mainland India with the eight Northeastern states.
● It is geographically constrained by **Nepal and** **Bhutan to its north** and **Bangladesh to its south**. This corridor is extremely narrow at its slimmest, just about 20–22 km wide. Because it acts as India’s only land link to the Northeast, this wedgelike placement between three neighbouring countries contributes to its vulnerability and immense strategic importance. **So, statement I is correct.**
● The **Mahananda and Teesta** transboundary rivers flow along the Siliguri region, forming important natural features of the corridor. **So, statement II is correct.**
● **The Tri-Shakti Corps, which is headquartered in Siliguri’s Sukna, has been in-charge of securing** **the corridor.** The Trishakti Corps also known as XXXIII Corps has a storied history, tracing its origins back to the British Indian Army’s XXXIII Corps, which played a pivotal role in World War II. Re-raised in 1960 at Shillong, the corps now operates under the Eastern Command, with its headquarters at Sukna, West Bengal. They responsible for securing the borders of Sikkim, Bhutan, and the Siliguri Corridor. **So,** **statement III is correct.**
● In response to shifting regional dynamics such as the political transition in Bangladesh, rising concerns over China’s activity near the Chumbi Valley, and broader strategic risks the Indian Army has recently established **three new garrison locations**. These are the **Lachit Borphukan Military Station in Dhubri** **(Assam)** and forward bases in **Kishanganj (Bihar)** and **Chopra (West Bengal)**. All three sites lie close to the India–Bangladesh border and are intended to enhance surveillance, boost readiness, and strengthen coordination with the Border Security Force. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Indian Army has recently established three new garrison locations near Siliguri Corridor.

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Major Dynasties of Early Medieval India
questions.questionText:
With reference to Hockey in India, consider the
following statements:
I. - The first Hockey Association was formed in the UK which provided the first official set of rules.
II.	 The establishment of a national federation
in 1925 marked the formal organisation of hockey in India.
III.	 Major Dhyan Chand was a member of the
Indian hockey team that won three successive Olympic gold medals.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
• The first version of modern-day field hockey was developed by the British sometime between the late 18th and early 19th century. It was introduced as a popular school game then and made its way to the Indian army during British rule in the 1850s.”
• In **1876, the first Hockey Association was formed in the UK which provided the first official set of** **rules.** The association continued only for six years however, it was later revived by nine founding member clubs. **So, statement I is correct.**
• In **1925**, **hockey got a formal structure in India** with the **creation of the national federation.** In 2009, it was replaced by Hockey India. It is the official governing body entrusted with overseeing all hockeyrelated activities—both men’s and women’s—across the country. It is affiliated with the International Hockey Federation (FIH), the Indian Olympic Association (IOA), and the Asian Hockey Federation (AHF). **So, statement II is correct.**
• Dhyan Chand was the first superstar of hockey, considered a wizard or magician of Hockey. He was the chief protagonist as **India won three consecutive Olympic hockey gold medals — Amsterdam 1928,** **Los Angeles 1932, and Berlin 1936.** He is said to have wowed the watching public with his sublime skills, intricate dribbling and gluttonous scoring ability. In all, Dhyan Chand played 12 Olympic matches, scoring 33 goals. **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** November 7 marked the 100th anniversary of the foundation of the Indian Hockey Federation – the country’s first governing body for the sport.

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.microTopic: India and the World (USA, Russia, Middle East, Africa, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard
to the United Nations Convention against Cybercrime:
I. - It was adopted by the United Nations General Assembly in 2024.
II.	 It was developed under the leadership of the
UN Office on Drugs and Crime.
III. It is the first universal, legally binding
international instrument dedicated to combat cybercrime.
IV. India has signed and ratified the treaty.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
●  The **United Nations Convention against Cybercrime** was adopted by the **UN General Assembly in** **December 2024** to strengthen global cooperation in combating cybercrime. It aims to promote international cooperation, strengthen measures to prevent and combat cybercrime, and support capacitybuilding, particularly for developing countries. It will enter into force 90 days after the 40th State deposits its ratification. **So, statement I is correct.**
● The Convention was developed under the leadership of the **UN Office on Drugs and Crime (UNODC)**,
which has long served as the lead agency for international cooperation on crime prevention and criminal justice. **So, statement II is correct.**
● The Convention is the **first universal, legally binding international instrument** aimed at combating cybercrime. It is open to all member states and legally obligates parties to strengthen domestic laws, cooperate across borders, and maintain a 24/7 network for rapid assistance. **So, statement III is correct.**
● **India has neither signed nor ratified the treaty** yet. Recently, the Supreme Court asked the Centre to take a call on ratifying. **So, statement IV is  not correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Recently, the Supreme Court asked the Centre to take a call on ratifying the United Nations Convention against Cybercrime.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to
the elections in India:
I. - The 42nd Constitutional Amendment Act, 1976 made during the Emergency extended the tenure of legislatures to six years.
II.	 The right to contest an election is neither a
fundamental right nor a common law right.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **42nd Constitutional Amendment Act**, passed during the Emergency in 1976, **increased the term** **of both the Lok Sabha and State Legislative Assemblies from five years to six years**. The extension was later reversed by the 44th Constitutional Amendment, restoring the original five-year term. This shows that the term of legislatures can be altered through a constitutional amendment. **So, statement I is** **correct.**
● The right to contest an election was **neither a fundamental right nor a common law right;** it's a right conferred by a statute. The Supreme Court in 2022 observed the right to contest an election was only a right provided by a legislation, **So, statement II is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** The Union Law Ministry told the Joint Parliamentary Committee that the proposed framework for simultaneous elections does not violate the Constitution’s basic structure or its federal character.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements about Euthanasia in India:
I. - Passive euthanasia was legally recognised by the Supreme Court in Common Cause vs Union of India (2018).
II.	 Active euthanasia is permitted in India only
for patients declared brain dead, with family consent.
III.	 As per the passive euthanasia guidelines, a
terminal illness is defined as an incurable condition with inevitable death.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● In the landmark judgment Common Cause vs Union of India (2018), a five-judge bench of the Supreme Court recognised the **right to die with dignity** as part of the fundamental right to life under Article 21. The Supreme Court permitted passive euthanasia (withdrawal of life support) and allowed individuals to draft a living will specifying that they should not be put on life support if they slip into an incurable coma or terminal condition. **So, statement I is correct.**
● **Active euthanasia remains illegal** in India under the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita (BNS), 2023 (Sections 100, 101, 103, 105), classified as culpable homicide or murder. Brain death cases allow withdrawal of life support under passive euthanasia guidelines with family consent, but this does not permit active euthanasia. **So, statement II is not correct.**
● Draft passive euthanasia guidelines prepared by AIIMS experts under Supreme Court directions define **terminal illness as an irreversible or incurable condition in which death is foreseeable.** They set out specific circumstances for withholding or withdrawing treatment, such as brainstem death, informed refusal, advance directives, and cases requiring approval from designated medical boards, ensuring that end-of-life decisions follow a clear and regulated process. **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Recently, the Supreme Court set up a medical board to examine matters related to passive euthanasia.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IPR, Institutions and Policies for IPR
questions.questionText:
With reference to the Indo-Tibetan Border Police,
consider the following statements:
I. - It was established under Indo-Tibetan Border Police Act of 1992.
II.	 The motto of the force is “Shaurya-Dridhata-
Karma Nishtha.”
III.	 It has actively participated in UN peacekeeping missions.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The Indo-Tibetan Border Police (ITBP) was **raised on 24 October 1962**, after the Sino-Indian War, to guard the India-China border. Initially constituted **under the CRPF Act, 1949**, it later received its own statute through the **ITBP Act of 1992**. **So, statement I is correct.**
● The official motto of the ITBP is **Shaurya-Dridhata-Karma Nishtha**,  translating to valor, determination, and devotion to duty. This motto is a guiding principle for all ITBP personnel (known as "**Himveers**"). **So, statement II is correct.**
● The ITBP has **actively participated in UN peacekeeping missions**, with personnel deployed in countries like Angola, Namibia, Cambodia, Bosnia and Herzegovina, Mozambique, and Kosovo. A National Centre for UNCIVPOL training has been established at ITBP Camp, Tigri, New Delhi, to provide systematic training to Indian police officers for UN deployments. **So, statement III is correct.**
● ITBP headquarters is located in New Delhi, under the Ministry of Home Affairs. It is not in Shimla, Himachal Pradesh, though the force operates extensively in the Himalayan regions. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Recently, the DG of ITBP, on the 64th Raising Day Parade, announced the establishment of 10 allwoman forward posts along the India-China border.

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.microTopic: Global Groupings and Agreements (FTA, Paris Agreement, NPT, QUAD, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements regarding the
Group of Eight (G8):
I. - It was formed as a response to the 1973 oil crisis.
II.	 Russia’s participation was suspended in 2014
following the illegal annexation of Crimea.
III.	 It does not have a permanent administrative
structure.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Group of Eight (G8)** was originally formed in 1976 as the G7, including the **US, UK, France,** **Germany, Italy, Canada, and Japan.** It was established as a platform for economic and financial cooperation in response to the 1973 energy crisis. Russia joined later in 1997, expanding it to the G8. This grouping was formed as a **forum of advanced economies** to discuss global economic and political issues. **So, statement I is correct.**
● After Russia annexed Crimea in March 2014, the other members suspended its participation, and the group has since functioned as the G7. **So, statement II is correct.**
● Compared to an international organization, the **G7 does not have a permanent administrative structure.** Each year, starting from the 1st of January, one of the Member States takes over the leadership of the Group on a rotating basis. The Nation holding the Presidency serves as a temporary secretariat and hosts the Group work and the Leaders’ Summit.  **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Recently, Europe has agreed to consider re-inviting Russia to the Group of Eight (G8) once the conflict in Ukraine is resolved.

questions.subject: Environment
questions.microTopic: Wildlife Conservation (Tiger, Elephant, Lion, Rhino, Crocodile, Vulture, Tortoise, Dolphin, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements regarding the International Big Cat Alliance (IBCA):
I. - It was launched by India on the occasion of the 50th anniversary of Project Tiger.
II. - It is a treaty-based intergovernmental international organisation and international legal entity.
III. 	Its objective is to ensure cooperation for the
conservation of seven big cats.
IV. 	India is scheduled to host the 'Global Big
Cats Summit' in 2026.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The International Big Cat Alliance (IBCA) was launched by the Prime Minister of India on 9 April 2023 at Mysuru, Karnataka, to commemorate the **50th anniversary of Project Tiger**. IBCA aims for the conservation of seven big cats, namely the tiger, lion, leopard, snow leopard, cheetah, jaguar and puma. **So, statements I and III are correct.**
● The IBCA is conceived as **a treaty-based intergovernmental organisation with international legal** **status**, enabling formal cooperation among member countries. Its institutional framework supports coordinated efforts in conservation, scientific research, knowledge exchange, and resource mobilisation for protecting big-cat species across their global ranges. **So, statement II is correct.**
● Membership in the International Big Cat Alliance is open to all countries interested in big-cat conservation. This inclusive approach enables broader global cooperation by allowing nations without these species to contribute through funding, technical expertise, research support, and international advocacy, thereby strengthening collective conservation efforts.
● At CoP30, the Indian Government announced India’s decision to host a ‘Global Big Cats Summit’ in New Delhi next year. **So, statement IV is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Recently, the Environment Minister declared that India would host a ‘Global Big Cats Summit’ in New Delhi next year.

questions.subject: Environment
questions.microTopic: Major Species (Keystone, Indicator, Invasive, Endemic, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following passage:
It is a tropical tree of the Malvaceae family, reaching up to 50 metres in height, and the tree is known by the Portuguese nomenclature. Revered as sacred, it is often described as the queen of the vast tropical forests that span nearly 40 per cent of Latin America, encompassing large regions of Brazil, Bolivia, Colombia, Ecuador, Peru, Venezuela, and beyond. It is:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The tree described is **Ceiba pentandra**, also known as the **Kapok tree**, a tropical tree of the **Malvaceae** **family** that can grow up to **50 metres tall.** It is known in **Portuguese nomenclature as** **Samauma**. This tree is revered as sacred and is often called the **queen of the tropical forests** that cover about **40% of** **Latin America, including Brazil, Bolivia, Colombia, Ecuador, Peru, Venezuela, and others.** The Samauma (Ceiba pentandra) has cultural and ecological significance and is widely spread across the tropical regions of Latin America. During the recent COP 30 summit, members of Brazil’s indigenous communities and residents highlighted various aspects of their culture, including the Samaúma tree. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** The Samaúma tree has become a powerful symbol of Indigenous protests against Amazonian deforestation during the COP30 climate conference in Belém, Brazil.

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.microTopic: Current Affairs Focus (Summits, Reports, Conflicts, Visits, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements regarding the recently held World Toilet Summit (WTS) 2025:
I. - The Summit coincided with the United Nations-designated World Toilet Day.
II. - It was hosted in India by Sulabh International, a social service organisation.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **World Toilet Summit (WTS)** is an annual event organized by the World Toilet Organization (WTO) to address the global sanitation crisis. The summit aims to break the taboo surrounding toilets and sanitation, promote better hygiene practices, and foster innovation in sanitation solutions.
● The Summit was held in New Delhi from November 19 to 21, 2025, coinciding with the United Nationsdesignated World Toilet Day. The United Nations marks November 19 annually as World Toilet Day to raise awareness about access to hygienic toilets and the human costs of unsafe sanitation. **So, statement** **I is correct.**
● The WTS 2025 was the 20th World Toilet Summit. It was held in Delhi, India, and was **hosted by Sulabh** **International.** The Summit coincided with the United Nations-designated World Toilet Day. **So,** **statement II is correct.**
● The theme of the World Toilet Day 2025 was ‘Sanitation: Collective Responsibility for Dignity and Planet’. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**World Toilet Organization:**
● It is a global non-profit organization established in 2001.
● It aims to enhance sanitation and advocates for safe and hygienic toilets worldwide.
● Through partnerships with governments, NGOs, and private sector organizations, the WTO implements innovative sanitation projects, promotes best practices, and empowers communities to address their sanitation needs.
● It was granted consultative status with the United Nations Economic and Social Council in 2013.
**Relevance:** Recently, the World Toilet Summit (WTS) 2025 was held in New Delhi.

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.microTopic: Current Affairs Focus (Summits, Reports, Conflicts, Visits, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. - Maharashtra has secured the first position in the ‘Best State’ category at the 6th National Water Awards.
II. - Rajasthan was the top-performing State under the Jal Sanchai Jan Bhagidari 1.0 initiative of the Ministry of Jal Shakti.
III. 	The 2024 Indira Gandhi Prize for Peace,
Disarmament and Development was awarded to former President of Chile, Michelle Bachelet.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Maharashtra has secured the first place in the ‘Best State’ category of the 6th National Water** **Awards.** The Award has been designed to raise awareness about the importance of water and encourage people to adopt best practices in its use. **So, statement I is correct.**
○ After Maharashtra, Gujarat secured the second position, and Haryana secured the third position. ○ The 6th National Water Awards for the year 2024 has been given in 10 categories like Best State, Best District, Best Village Panchayat, Best Urban Local Body, Best School or College etc.
● **Telangana has secured the top position in the Jal Sanchai Jan Bhagidari (JSJB) 1.0 initiative** of the Union Ministry of Jal Shakti, emerging as the best-performing State in rainwater conservation. Chhattisgarh and Rajasthan secured the second and third positions respectively. **So, statement II is not** **correct.**
○ During 2024-25, Telangana undertook 5,20,362 rainwater harvesting works, the highest in the country. ○ The Jal Sanchai Jan Bhagidari (JSJB) initiative was in 2024, to promote community-led water stewardship through a whole-of-government and whole-of-society model. States were tasked with creating a minimum of 10,000 artificial recharge and storage structures, while municipal corporations were required to complete at least 10,000, and urban local bodies (ULBs) 2,000 each.
● The **Indira Gandhi Prize for Peace, Disarmament and Development for 2024 was given to Michelle** **Bachelet,** the first and only woman President of Chile and a former chief of UN Human Rights. **So,** **statement III is correct.**
○ The prize was instituted in 1985 in the memory of the former Prime Minister Indira Gandhi. It is conferred every year by Indira Gandhi Memorial Trust which consists of an award of Rs.10 million and a trophy with a citation. It shall be awarded annually to a person or organization and can be awarded without any distinction of nationality, race or religion. The awardee is selected by a jury of eminent persons. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Recently, the Indira Gandhi Prize for Peace, Disarmament and Development for 2024 was given to Michelle Bachelet.

questions.subject: Environment
questions.microTopic: Wildlife Conservation (Tiger, Elephant, Lion, Rhino, Crocodile, Vulture, Tortoise, Dolphin, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements regarding the Dugong, also known as the Sea Cow:
I. - It is a herbivorous mammal.
II. - It usually thrives in cold and deep ocean waters.
III. 	It is listed as ‘vulnerable’ on the IUCN Red
List of threatened species.
IV. 	In India, it is naturally found only in the Gulf
of Mannar and the Palk Bay.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The Dugong (Dugong dugon), also called the sea cow, is a mammal. **The mammals are exclusively** **herbivorous**. Seagrass meadows are their main source of food — the reason why they are also called sea cows. **So, statement I is correct.**
○ Dugongs play a crucial role in maintaining healthy seagrass ecosystems. For instance, when they graze seagrass, the leaves are pruned and old shoots are uprooted.
● Dugongs **inhabit shallow, and warm coastal waters**. **Dugong do not prefer cold water.** If the water gets cold, less than 17 degrees Centigrade, they  travel to warmer areas. **So, statement II is not correct.**
● Currently, the IUCN, which compiles the official **Red List of endangered species**, lists **Dugongs as** **‘vulnerable’.** In India, they are listed under Schedule I of the Wild Life (Protection) Act, 1972, which grants the species the highest legal protection against hunting and trade. Also, dugongs are listed in Appendix I of the Convention on International Trade in Endangered Species of Wild Fauna and Flora (CITES). **So, statement III is correct.**
● In India, **dugongs are primarily found in the Gulf of Kutch, the Gulf of Mannar–Palk Bay region** **(located between India and Sri Lanka), and the Andaman and Nicobar Islands.** In India, the largest population exists in the Gulf of Mannar and in Palk Bay. **So, statement IV is not correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** A new report launched at the International Union for Conservation of Nature (IUCN) Conservation Congress, in Abu Dhabi, United Arab Emirates highlighted the growing threat to the dugong population in India.

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.microTopic: Security and Strategic Issues (Terrorism, Cyber, Maritime, Exercises, etc.)
questions.questionText:
With reference to India, consider the following pairs:
Operation Reason for the launch
I. - Crystal Fortress To fight against drug trafficking
II. - Sagar Bandhu To evacuate Indians from Yemen
III. - MED MAX To tackle illegal phar­ maceutical drug trade.
IV. - Mahadev To provide humani­ tarian assistance to Sri Lanka
How many of the above-given pairs are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● A joint operation named **“Crystal Fortress”** by the Narcotics Control Bureau and Delhi Police has recently dismantled a major transnational methamphetamine cartel, seizing 328 kg of drugs worth Rs 262 crore. The coordinated intelligence-led crackdown was codenamed Operation Crystal Fortress. **The joint** **operation was aimed at disrupting a synthetic drug syndicate** believed to be controlled by a foreignbased kingpin, already wanted in connection with last year’s 83 kg cocaine seizure in Delhi. **So, pair I is** **correctly matched.**
○ The operation drew its name from “Crystal”, referring to methamphetamine, and “fortress”, denoting the secured area where it was hidden, similar to a fort. Methamphetamine is also known as the “Ice” drug because of its white, crystal-like appearance and its high demand at rave parties.
● Cyclone Ditwah has caused severe flooding, loss of life and widespread disruption across Sri Lanka. Thus, **India launched Operation Sagar Bandhu on 28 November 2025 to provide urgent Search & Rescue** **and Humanitarian Assistance & Disaster Relief (HADR) support to Sri Lanka**. Indian is providing humanitarian assistance to Sri Lanka through the airlifting of stranded victims, providing medical assistance and helping re-establish connectivity. **So, pair II is not correctly matched.**
● **Operation MED MAX was one of the crackdowns carried out by NCB against the illegal** **pharmaceutical drug trade**. The operation successfully dismantled a transnational drug trafficking syndicate that used encrypted digital platforms, drop shipping models, and cryptocurrency to smuggle controlled medicines across four continents. **So, pair III is correctly matched.**
● **Operation Mahadev was a major anti-terror operation** launched in July 2025 following the deadly Pahalgam attack in Jammu and Kashmir. It **aimed to eliminate high-value terrorists behind the attack.** Operation Mahadev was conducted jointly by the Army, CRPF, and Jammu and Kashmir Police. The terrorists involved in the Pahalgam massacre were killed during 'Operation Mahadev'. **So, pair IV is not** **correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**International Day Against Drug Abuse 2025**
● The UN General Assembly has declared June 26 as International Day against Drug Abuse and Illicit Trafficking to encourage action and collaboration towards the goal of a drug-free world.
● The theme for 2025 International Day Against Drug Abuse is “Break the Cycle. #StopOrganizedCrime“. It emphasises the significance of focused long-term action to break the cycle of organised crime and drug trafficking.
**Relevance:** A joint operation by the Narcotics Control Bureau and Delhi Police has recently dismantled a major transnational methamphetamine cartel under operation Crystal Fortress.

questions.subject: Environment
questions.microTopic: Biodiversity (IUCN, WWF, CBD, Cartagena, Nagoya, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to Kunming Biodiversity Fund (KBF):
I. - It is a Multi-Partner Trust Fund established by the Ministry of Ecology and Environment of China and the United Nations Environment Program.
II. - It was launched during the 15th Conference of the Parties to the Convention on Biological Diversity.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Kunming Biodiversity Fund (KBF)** is a Multi-Partner Trust Fund (MPTF) established by the **Ministry of Ecology and Environment (MEE) of China** and the **United Nations Environment** **Program (UNEP),** with the Secretariat of the Convention on Biological Diversity (SCBD) and United Nations Development Programme (UNDP). **So, statement I is correct.**
● The KBF aims to facilitate the successful implementation of the **Kunming-Montreal Global** **Biodiversity Framework (KMGBF)** at the regional, national, subnational and local level by supporting developing countries to accelerate and upscale their actions for the National Biodiversity Strategies and Action Plans (NBSAPs).
● It was launched in **2021** during Part 1 of the **15th Conference of the Parties to the Convention on** **Biological Diversity**. China committed an initial **1.5 billion yuan (around $200 million)** to support developing nations in protecting biodiversity and advancing the implementation of the **Kunming-** **Montreal Global Biodiversity Framework (KMGBF)**. **So, statement II is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Recently, seven countries have secured $5.8 million from the Kunming Biodiversity Fund to enhance nature-friendly agriculture.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Straits, Channels, Oceans, Seas, Gulfs, Bays
questions.questionText: Which one of the following territories is located within the Caribbean Sea?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Curacao is an Island country located in the southern** **Caribbean Sea.** It is just 40 miles north of the coast of
Venezuela and South America. The country includes the main island of Curacao and the island of Klein Curacao.
● The island became a country of the Kingdom of the Netherlands in 2010, following the dissolution of the Netherlands Antilles. Currently, Curacao is administered by an independent government established by the people of Curacao and is fully autonomous in most matters, except those outlined in the Charter for the Kingdom of the Netherlands. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Recently, the Curacao Island became the smallest ever nation to qualify for the 2026 FIFA World Cup.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.microTopic: Money (Evolution, Types, Digital Currency, Crypto, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to Stablecoins:
I. - They are cryptocurrencies backed by reserves such as fiat currencies, commodities, or other crypto assets.
II. - Unlike central bank digital currencies, they are not issued by the central banks.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● According to the Bank for International Settlements, stablecoin can be defined as a **cryptocurrency that** **aims to maintain a stable value relative to a specified asset,** or a pool or basket of assets. They are blockchain-based digital assets designed to maintain a **consistent value over time.** They are **backed by** **reserves** such as **fiat currencies, commodities, or other crypto assets.** **So, statement I is correct.**
● **Stablecoins** are **not** issued **by governments or central banks**. They are created and managed by **private** **companies**, crypto platforms, or fintech firms. In contrast, **Central Bank Digital Currencies (CBDCs)** are official digital currencies issued by **central banks** like RBI’s e-rupee. **So, statement II is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** China's central bank took a tough stance on virtual currencies vowing to crack down on illegal activities involving stablecoins.

questions.subject: International Relations
